The latch shifts, grinding open with a sound like nails on stone.\n\nToo loud.\n\nThe voices below stop.\n\n“Second floor. Someone’s up there.”\n\nTiffani shoves Jhace through the hatch, squeezes in after him, and pulls it shut. They crouch in darkness—tight space, bodies pressed together, breath synchronized. The hatch is shallow. If the Sanitists check thoroughly, they’ll find it.\n\nJhace feels Tiffani’s warmth against his side. Her hand finds his in the dark, fingers threading through his, gripping tight.\n\n“Don’t move,” she breathes.\n\nHe doesn’t.\n\nFootsteps ascend the stairs. Two inspectors, maybe three. They reach the fifth-floor landing and stop. Jhace hears the scrape of boots on wood, the rustle of fabric as someone kneels to examine the joists.\n\n“Fresh velding. Recent. Still warm.”\n\n“Illegal repairs. Mark it.”\n\n“Should we search?”\n\nA pause. Long enough that Jhace’s lungs start to burn from shallow breathing.\n\n“No. Whoever did this is gone. We’ll post the violation and escalate to enforcement.” Boot steps retreat. “Next building.”\n\nThe voices fade. The stairwell goes quiet.\n\nTiffani doesn’t move. Neither does Jhace. They wait—three minutes, five—until the footsteps are gone completely and only the hum of heat pipes remains.\n\nThen Tiffani exhales, slow and shaking. “That was close.”\n\n“Too close.” Jhace shifts slightly, realizing how cramped the space is. Their knees touch. Her shoulder presses against his chest. He can feel her heartbeat—fast, adrenaline-sharp.\n\n“We should wait another ten minutes,” Tiffani says. “Make sure they’re gone.”\n\n“Agreed.”\n\nSilence settles between them. Not uncomfortable—just aware. Jhace is hyperconscious of her proximity, the way her breath catches slightly when she moves, the warmth of her hand still gripping his.\n\n“Jhace,” she says quietly.\n\n“Yeah?”\n\n“That technique I showed you. Sharing the vein. Don’t try it with anyone else.”\n\nHe frowns. “Why not?”\n\n“Because it requires trust. Complete trust. If the other person panics, or pushes too hard, or tries to take control…” She trails off. “You both collapse. And one of you might not recover.”\n\nJhace processes that. “You trusted me.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Why?”\n\nTiffani turns her head slightly, and even in the darkness, he feels her gaze on him. “Because you’ve never taken more than you could carry. Even when it cost you everything.”\n\nSomething twists in Jhace’s chest—not pain, something else. Warmth. Recognition. The realization that she sees him—not just his capacity, but his limits, his failures, the way he bleeds himself empty trying to hold the city together.\n\n“Tiffani,” he says, voice rough.\n\n“Yeah?”\n\n“I don’t know how to do this.”\n\n“Do what?”\n\n“Trust someone.” He exhales shakily. “I’m not good at it.”\n\n“I know.” She shifts closer, and her forehead brushes his shoulder. “But you’re trying. That counts.”\n\nThey sit in the dark, breathing the same air, bodies pressed close in a space too small for walls. Jhace feels the tremor in his hands ease slightly—not gone, never gone, but bearable.\n\n“We should go,” Tiffani says eventually, but she doesn’t move.\n\n“Yeah.” Jhace doesn’t move either.\n\nAnother minute passes. Then Tiffani squeezes his hand once, releases it, and pushes the hatch open.\n\nThey emerge into the stairwell—empty now, silent except for the hum of heat pipes beneath cobblestones. The beam Jhace braced earlier holds. The joists Tiffani reinforced are stable. The tenement leans four degrees, same as before, but it won’t collapse tonight.\n\nTomorrow’s a different question.\n\nThey descend the stairs in silence, slipping out through a side exit into the alley behind the building. The night is cold. The bells ring eighth hour. The Dregs sleep, or try to, beneath cracked roofs and failing supports.\n\nJhace and Tiffani walk side by side toward the storage alcove they’ve been using as a base—hidden, defensible, stocked with tools and stolen supplies. They don’t speak. Don’t need to.\n\nWhen they reach the alcove, Tiffani stops at the entrance, turning to face him. “Get some rest. Your hands are shaking.”\n\n“So are yours.”\n\nShe smiles—brief, tired. “We’re a matched set.”\n\nJhace almost laughs. “Romantic.”\n\n“Accurate.” She steps closer, close enough that he can see the faint scar above her collarbone, the exhaustion carved into her face. “Tomorrow we tackle the northern scaffold. Bram says it’s worse than the last one.”\n\n“Of course it is.”\n\nTiffani reaches up, brushing her thumb along his jaw—brief contact, deliberate. “You don’t have to do this alone anymore.”\n\nJhace catches her wrist gently, holding her hand against his face. “I know.”\n\nShe doesn’t pull away. Neither does he.\n\nThey stand there, in the darkness of an alcove that smells like rust and old tools, holding each other’s gaze. No words. Just presence.\n\nThen Tiffani steps back, releasing his wrist. “Sleep. I’ll take first watch.”\n\n“You need rest too.”\n\n“I’ll rest when you do.”\n\nJhace knows better than to argue. He moves deeper into the alcove, finding the makeshift bedroll they’ve been sharing—two thin blankets, a rolled coat for a pillow. He collapses onto it, exhaustion hitting him all at once.\n\nTiffani sits at the entrance, back against the wall, stylus resting across her knees. She watches the alley, alert, competent.\n\nJhace closes his eyes, feeling the tremor in his hands slowly ease. He thinks about her fingers threading through his in the dark. About trust. About partnership.\n\nAbout the fact that he might be falling for someone who knows exactly how broken he is and stays anyway.\n\nHe doesn’t know what to do with that.\n\nSo he does what he always does—holds the thought close, and keeps working.\n\nTomorrow, they’ll brace another scaffold. Tomorrow, they’ll risk another patrol. Tomorrow, the Dregs will wake beneath structures held together by sweat and stubbornness.\n\nBut tonight, Jhace sleeps beside someone who shares the weight.\n\nAnd for the first time in years, the tremor in his hands doesn’t wake him.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 5\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nShared vein technique: Tiffani threads Form resonance through Jhace’s Wholeness, redistributing load (advanced dual-principle integration, requires complete trust)\n\nSanitist patrol: Illegal repair discovered, violation posted, enforcement escalated (building marked for clearance)\n\nService hatch escape: Narrow space forces physical proximity, builds intimacy through crisis\n\nHeat pipes: Background hum beneath cobblestones, constant presence (origin still uninvestigated)\n\nTiffani’s teacher reference: “Someone who’s gone” taught her that craft requires shared weight\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nTenement eastern stairwell: Beam braced, joists reinforced, structure stabilized (temporarily)\n\nSanitist violation posted: Building marked for enforcement escalation, residents’ eviction timeline shortened\n\nShared vein technique established: Jhace and Tiffani now have coordinated capacity, but technique is dangerous with untrusted partners\n\nStorage alcove: Established as shared base—tools, bedroll, defensible position\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nBody: Jhace—hand tremor worsens after 12-hour work day, chronic tendon pain, exhaustion\n\nBody: Tiffani—rib scar flares during shared vein technique, chronic strain visible\n\nSocial: Building residents now at higher risk—Sanitist violation accelerates clearance timeline\n\nTime: Night work limits hours available for repairs, increases detection risk\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nDusk to eighth bell: Illegal stairwell repair under Sanitist patrol threat\n\nEighth bell: Return to storage alcove, Tiffani takes first watch\n\nNight: Jhace sleeps without tremor for first time in years (trust allows rest)\n\nNew Constraint (Persists 2–3 Chapters): Shared vein technique unlocked—Jhace and Tiffani can now redistribute craft load between them, but the technique requires complete trust and simultaneous resonance. If either panics or overextends, both collapse. This creates tactical advantage (Chapter 6: dome repair becomes feasible) but also vulnerability (Chapter 7: if one is injured, the other cannot work alone at previous capacity). Additionally, Sanitist patrols have intensified—illegal repairs now result in immediate violation postings, shortening eviction timelines. This pressures Chapter 6 (Consent as Craft—must teach technique to others or work becomes unsustainable) and Chapter 7 (The Dome Cracks Wider—crisis requires shared vein under combat conditions).\n\nRomance Micro-Act (Cluster 2, Act 1 of 2): Jhace chooses to trust Tiffani with shared vein technique (allows her resonance through his craft channels). Tiffani chooses to brush his jaw after escape (deliberate physical contact, vulnerability shown). Both choose proximity over distance in alcove—sleeping beside each other, accepting partnership as intimacy.\n\nCHAPTER 6 — Consent as Craft\n\nTiffani POV\n\nJhace’s hands are shaking again.\n\nTiffani watches from three meters away as he presses his palms flat against the northern scaffold anchor—rusted bolt, corroded joint, metal that should’ve been replaced a year ago. His right hand is wrapped in fresh bandages. His left trembles visibly.\n\nHe’s been working since dawn. It’s past noon now. Six hours straight, no rest, and the tremor is back.\n\n“Jhace,” Tiffani says quietly. “Take a break.”\n\n“Can’t.” His voice is rough, strained. “This anchor fails, the whole western face collapses. Forty families.”\n\n“I know. But you’re overextending.”\n\n“I’m holding.”\n\nTiffani steps closer, close enough to see the sweat sheeting down his face despite the cold, the way his jaw clenches with each breath. He’s pouring Wholeness into the bolt—raw, desperate, unguided—forcing the metal to stay intact through sheer refusal to let it fail.\n\nIt’s not sustainable. And he knows it.\n\n“Let me help,” Tiffani says.\n\nJhace doesn’t respond. Doesn’t look at her. Just keeps holding, breath ragged, fingers splayed against metal that’s already screaming for release.\n\nTiffani kneels beside him, close enough that their shoulders touch. She pulls her velding stylus from her belt, but she doesn’t press it against the anchor. Not yet.\n\n“Jhace. Look at me.”\n\nHe turns his head slightly, eyes unfocused, exhaustion carved into every line of his face.\n\n“I’m going to thread through your resonance,” Tiffani says. “Same technique as last night. But I need your consent. Full consent. If you’re not ready, or if you don’t trust me, this won’t work.”\n\nJhace stares at her for a long beat. Then he nods, once.\n\n“Say it,” Tiffani says. “I need to hear you say it.”\n\nJhace exhales shakily. “I consent. Do it.”\n\nTiffani presses her stylus against the anchor, just above where Jhace’s hands rest. She finds the vein—thrashing, rupturing in slow motion—and opens herself to it. Not forcing. Not pushing. Just… inviting the stress to redistribute through her channels instead of his.\n\nThe vein responds.\n\nSlowly at first. The stress seeps into her stylus, threading through her craft channels, and Tiffani guides it—not away from the anchor, but into dormant pathways within the metal, distributing the load across the joint instead of concentrating it at the failure point.\n\nJhace’s breath eases.\n\nThe tremor in his hands slows.\n\n“I feel it,” he whispers.\n\n“Good.” Tiffani’s voice stays even, controlled, though her ribs are already starting to ache. “Now breathe with me. In… out. Slow.”\n\nThey breathe together—synchronized, measured—and the vein settles. The anchor stabilizes. The bolt stops screaming.\n\nTiffani pulls the stylus back, flexing her fingers. Her rib scar is on fire—sharp, immediate pain radiating through her chest. She presses her free hand against her ribs, breathing shallow.\n\nJhace sees it. “You’re hurt.”\n\n“I’m fine.” Tiffani straightens, forcing herself upright. “The anchor’s stable. But it won’t hold permanently. We need to reinforce the base.”\n\n“You just—” Jhace stares at her, awe and concern mingling in his expression. “You took half the load. I felt it shift.”\n\n“That’s the technique. Shared vein.” Tiffani adjusts her toolbelt, trying to mask the pain. “It works because we trust each other. But it costs. Always.”\n\nJhace reaches out, catching her wrist gently. “Show me.”\n\nTiffani hesitates. Then she exhales slowly and lifts the edge of her shirt, just enough to reveal the scar—old, jagged, running along her left ribs. The skin around it is flushed, inflamed from the resonance backlash.\n\nJhace stares at the scar, then looks up at her face. “When did that happen?”\n\n“Years ago. Bridge support collapse in Strathmoor.” Tiffani lowers her shirt. “I tried to veld the joint mid-failure. It detonated. The foreman died three meters away. I survived with this.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a long moment. Then he says, “And you still do this work.”\n\n“Because someone has to.” Tiffani meets his gaze. “And because I learned how to do it better. Precision doesn’t prevent failure. But it makes survival more likely.”\n\nJhace nods slowly. He doesn’t let go of her wrist. “Teach me.”\n\n“Teach you what?”\n\n“How to work like you do. Precise. Controlled. Not just brute force and stubbornness.” He flexes his free hand, grimacing at the tremor. “I can’t keep doing this. My hands… they’re failing. I need another way.”\n\nTiffani studies his face—exhaustion, determination, vulnerability. He’s asking for more than technique. He’s asking for trust. For partnership that goes deeper than crisis response.\n\n“It’s not easy,” she says quietly. “Velding requires control most craftspeople don’t have. And it’s slow. You won’t save everyone. You’ll have to choose who gets your time.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“Do you?” Tiffani’s voice sharpens slightly. “Because you’ve been trying to hold this entire district together alone. And it’s killing you.”\n\nJhace doesn’t flinch. “Then teach me how to hold it smarter.”\n\nTiffani exhales, relief and dread mingling. She releases his wrist and pulls a second stylus from her belt—narrower, etched with pressure glyphs Jhace hasn’t seen before.\n\n“First lesson,” Tiffani says, pressing the stylus into his left hand. “You don’t force the vein. You open pathways for it to follow. Energy takes the path of least resistance. You give it options.”\n\nJhace turns the stylus over in his palm, studying the etchings. “How do I open a pathway?”\n\n“Breath. Intention. Trust.” Tiffani kneels beside the anchor again, gesturing for him to join her. “Watch.”\n\nShe presses her stylus against the metal, tracing the vein with deliberate slowness. As she exhales, the stylus glows faint amber, and the stress channel responds—not thrashing, not collapsing, just… easing into a new configuration.\n\n“You’re not pushing energy into the vein,” Jhace says, watching closely. “You’re guiding it.”\n\n“Exactly.” Tiffani pulls the stylus back. “Now you try.”\n\nJhace hesitates, then presses the borrowed stylus against the anchor. He finds the vein—easier now that Tiffani’s already stabilized it—and exhales, slow and controlled.\n\nNothing happens.\n\nHe tries again. Still nothing.\n\n“You’re holding your breath,” Tiffani says. “Let it go. Completely.”\n\nJhace exhales fully, and the stylus flickers—faint, brief, but real.\n\nThe vein shifts. Not dramatically. Just a small redistribution of stress, barely noticeable. But it’s enough.\n\nJhace stares at the stylus, eyes wide. “It worked.”\n\n“It did.” Tiffani smiles—brief, genuine. “That’s the foundation. Everything else builds from there.”\n\nThey work together through the afternoon, redistributing stress across the anchor joint. Tiffani guides; Jhace follows, learning the rhythm of velding through repetition. His hands still shake, but less. The tremor doesn’t vanish, but it becomes… manageable.\n\nAt some point, the work shifts. They’re not just stabilizing an anchor. They’re learning each other—how to move in sync, when to push and when to yield, how to trust someone else with weight you’ve always carried alone.\n\nTiffani feels it in her chest—not just the rib pain, but something warmer. Trust. Partnership. The beginnings of something she hasn’t felt in years.\n\nWhen the anchor finally stabilizes completely, they sit back, breathing hard, hands still touching the metal.\n\n“You’re better at this than you think,” Tiffani says quietly.\n\n“I’m slower than you.”\n\n“You’re learning. That’s different.” She shifts, leaning into him slightly. “Most people can’t adapt mid-crisis. You did. That’s rare.”\n\nJhace turns his head, meeting her gaze. “Who taught you?”\n\nTiffani hesitates. She’s deflected this question before, but sitting here, exhausted and vulnerable, she can’t find the energy to lie.\n\n“Her name was Marisse,” Tiffani says quietly. “She was a master velder in Strathmoor. Taught me everything I know about precision and control. Believed that craft should never force—only guide.”\n\n“What happened to her?”\n\n“She died holding a transit bridge. Refused to let go even when the vein detonated.” Tiffani’s voice flattens. “I watched. Couldn’t reach her in time. And I’ve been carrying that failure ever since.”\n\nJhace doesn’t say anything. He just reaches out, threading his fingers through hers, gripping tight.\n\nThey sit in silence, hands intertwined, sharing the weight of memory and exhaustion.\n\n“Thank you,” Jhace says finally.\n\n“For what?”\n\n“For teaching me. For trusting me.” He squeezes her hand gently. “For staying.”\n\nTiffani looks at him—scarred hands, exhausted eyes, stubborn refusal to quit—and feels something crack open in her chest. Not breaking. Opening.\n\n“I’m not going anywhere,” she says.\n\nJhace smiles—tired, real. “Good.”\n\nThey stand, dusting off their knees, and walk back toward the storage alcove. The sun is setting. The bells ring sixth hour. The Dregs settle into evening routines—cooking over heat pipes, mending torn fabric, children playing on patched stairs.\n\nThe world is still broken. But for now, it holds.\n\nThat night, they sit in the alcove, side by side, tools spread between them. Tiffani shows Jhace how to clean velding styli properly—careful, methodical, a ritual of care and maintenance.\n\nJhace watches her hands move, learning the rhythm. Then he looks up at her face.\n\n“Tiffani.”\n\n“Yeah?”\n\n“I don’t know how to do this.”\n\n“Do what?”\n\n“Be close to someone.” He exhales shakily. “I’m not good at it.”\n\nTiffani sets down the stylus, turning to face him fully. “You’re doing fine.”\n\n“Am I?”\n\nShe reaches out, brushing her thumb along his jaw—deliberate, gentle. “You asked for my help. You trusted me with your craft channels. You’re learning techniques that require vulnerability.” She leans closer. “That’s not failing. That’s trying.”\n\nJhace catches her wrist, holding her hand against his face. “I don’t want to break this.”\n\n“You won’t.”\n\n“How do you know?”\n\n“Because we’re both too stubborn to let it collapse.” Tiffani smiles. “And because I’ve seen how you hold things together. You don’t quit.”\n\nJhace closes his eyes, breathing slow. Then he pulls her closer, forehead resting against hers.\n\nThey sit like that, in the darkness of an alcove that smells like rust and old tools, breathing the same air. No words. Just presence.\n\nEventually, Tiffani pulls back, just enough to see his face. “Get some rest. Tomorrow we tackle the market dome again. Thirty-one ribs left.”\n\n“Thirty-one.” Jhace almost laughs. “We’ll never finish.”\n\n“We will.” Tiffani stands, extending her hand. “Together.”\n\nJhace takes her hand, letting her pull him upright. They move deeper into the alcove, finding the shared bedroll. They collapse onto it, exhausted, bodies pressed close in the narrow space.\n\nTiffani feels Jhace’s hand find hers in the dark, fingers threading together.\n\n“Tiffani,” he whispers.\n\n“Yeah?”\n\n“Thank you.”\n\nShe doesn’t ask what for. She just squeezes his hand and closes her eyes.\n\nAnd for the first time in years, neither of them sleeps alone.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 6\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nShared vein technique: Tiffani teaches Jhace velding fundamentals, threading resonance through borrowed stylus (consent explicitly given, technique ritualized)\n\nVelding pedagogy: “Open pathways, don’t force”—core principle demonstrated through breath, intention, trust\n\nTiffani’s teacher revealed: Marisse (master velder, Strathmoor, died holding transit bridge)\n\nRib scar visibility: Tiffani shows Jhace the injury, contextualizes cost of precision work\n\nHeat pipes: Background presence, no investigation (bells mark time passage)\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nBorrowed velding stylus: Jhace now possesses tool with pressure glyphs, can perform basic velding\n\nNorthern scaffold anchor: Stabilized through shared vein technique, temporarily safe\n\nPedagogical partnership: Tiffani formally teaching Jhace velding—shifts dynamic from equals to mentor/student (adds tension)\n\nMarisse memorial: Named teacher, establishes Tiffani’s lineage and loss\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nBody: Jhace—hand tremor worsens after 6-hour hold, exhaustion visible\n\nBody: Tiffani—rib scar flares during shared vein, pain visible and acknowledged\n\nEmotional: Tiffani reveals teacher’s death, vulnerability shown (trust deepens)\n\nTime: Full afternoon spent teaching, reduces available repair hours\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nDawn to noon: Jhace braces anchor alone (6 hours, overextension)\n\nAfternoon: Velding lesson, anchor stabilization\n\nSixth bell: Return to alcove, tool maintenance ritual\n\nNight: Shared rest, hands intertwined\n\nNew Constraint (Persists 2–3 Chapters): Jhace is now learning velding—slower, more precise, but less forceful than his natural brasting. This creates tactical advantage (can perform delicate repairs previously impossible) but also limitation (velding takes longer, can’t respond to rapid crises with same speed). In Chapter 7 (The Dome Cracks Wider), this constraint will pressure him: a crisis requires immediate brasting, but Tiffani insists on velding, creating conflict. Additionally, the pedagogical shift (Tiffani as teacher) introduces power imbalance into the partnership—Jhace must reconcile learning from someone he views as equal, while Tiffani must avoid condescension. This tension surfaces in Chapter 7 when Jhace defies her instruction during emergency.\n\nRomance Micro-Act (Cluster 2, Act 2 of 2): Jhace chooses to ask for teaching (vulnerability shown through request for help). Tiffani chooses to reveal Marisse (trust through shared grief). Both choose physical proximity—forehead to forehead, hands intertwined, sleeping together without sexual intent (intimacy as partnership confirmation).\n\nCHAPTER 7 — The Dome Cracks Wider\n\nJhace POV\n\nThe glass detonates at second bell.\n\nJhace is halfway up the dome’s eastern rib when he hears it—a sharp crack, like bone breaking, followed by the high-frequency shriek of glass fracturing under catastrophic stress. He looks up and sees the stress-trace spider across the crown, branching faster than he can track.\n\nNew sabotage.\n\nSomeone patterned fresh risers into the dome while they slept. Probably during the night patrol gap. The structure they spent two weeks stabilizing is failing again—worse than before.\n\n“Tiffani!” Jhace shouts.\n\nShe’s on the opposite side of the dome, thirty meters away, already moving. She sees the fracture pattern and her face goes pale.\n\n“Get down!” she yells. “Now!”\n\nJhace doesn’t move. He presses both hands against the rib beneath him, finding the vein. If he can brace this section—hold it long enough for Tiffani to redistribute the load—maybe they can stop the cascade.\n\nThe vein screams.\n\nIt’s worse than the scaffold anchor in Chapter 1. Worse than the stairwell beam in Chapter 5. This isn’t structural fatigue—it’s active sabotage, designed to cause explosive failure. Whoever patterned these risers knew exactly what they were doing.\n\nJhace floods the rib with Wholeness—raw, desperate, everything he has. The glass resists. It doesn’t want to be healed. It wants to shatter.\n\n“Jhace, let go!” Tiffani’s voice cuts through the noise. “You can’t brast glass under active failure!”\n\nHe doesn’t let go.\n\nHe finds the fracture line and holds—forcing the vein to stay intact through sheer refusal to let two hundred people lose their market. The tremor in his hands is back, violent, and his right palm splits open—fresh blood soaking through the bandages.\n\nThe rib stabilizes. Barely.\n\n“Now!” Jhace shouts. “Redistribute while I’m holding!”\n\nTiffani hesitates—just a second, but he sees it. She doesn’t think this will work. She thinks he’s overextending, killing himself for a repair that’s already lost.\n\nBut she moves anyway.\n\nShe climbs fast, stylus glowing amber in her left hand, and reaches the crown. She presses the stylus against the stress-trace and guides—not forcing, not pushing, just opening pathways for the vein to follow.\n\nIt’s working. The fracture slows. The shriek fades to a low hum.\n\nThen Jhace’s grip falters.\n\nHis right hand seizes—tendon splits tearing wider, fingers locking into a claw. Pain floods through his arm, white-hot and immediate. He tries to hold, tries to keep the vein stable, but his hand won’t work.\n\nThe rib shudders.\n\n“Jhace!” Tiffani’s voice is sharp, panicked.\n\nHe lets go.\n\nThe vein collapses inward, and the dome shrieks—every rib fracturing simultaneously, stress cascading faster than either of them can counter.\n\nTiffani doesn’t hesitate. She throws Form into the crown—not velding, not guiding, just raw structural force—and locks the ribs into place with techniques Jhace has never seen.\n\nThe dome stops moving.\n\nEvery rib freezes mid-fracture, held in suspension by geometry that shouldn’t be possible. The stress-traces don’t fade—they’re still there, visible and lethal—but the structure isn’t collapsing.\n\nNot yet.\n\nJhace stares at Tiffani across the dome. She’s breathing hard, face pale, stylus glowing so bright it hurts to look at. Her hands are shaking.\n\n“Where did you learn that?” Jhace asks, voice rough.\n\nTiffani doesn’t answer. She just pulls the stylus back and climbs down, fast and deliberate.\n\nJhace follows, cradling his right hand against his chest. Blood drips through the bandages, leaving dark spots on cobblestone. By the time he reaches ground level, Tiffani is already kneeling beside a fallen rigger—Kylar, pinned under a beam that sheared free during the cascade.\n\nKylar’s breathing is shallow. There’s blood soaking through his shirt. A lot of blood.\n\nJhace drops to his knees beside them. “Let me—”\n\n“No.” Tiffani’s voice is flat, controlled. “You’re overextended. If you try to heal him now, you’ll both collapse.”\n\n“I can—”\n\n“You can’t.” She presses her hands against Kylar’s chest, finding the injury. “Broken ribs. Punctured lung. I can stabilize him, but you need to get back.”\n\nJhace doesn’t move. He watches Tiffani work—precise, methodical, hands glowing faint amber as she threads Form through Kylar’s ribs, locking them into place. Not healing. Just… holding. Buying time for the healers to arrive.\n\nIt’s velding. Advanced velding. Techniques that take years to learn.\n\nJhace has known Tiffani for three weeks. She told him she learned from her teacher, Marisse, but she never said how much she learned. Never showed the full extent of her capacity.\n\nUntil now.\n\nKylar’s breathing eases. The blood stops spreading. He’s still unconscious, but stable.\n\nTiffani pulls her hands back, flexing her fingers. Her rib scar is visible through her shirt—flushed, inflamed. She’s in pain, but she doesn’t show it.\n\n“Who taught you that?” Jhace asks quietly.\n\nTiffani looks at him. Her eyes are tired. Guarded. “Someone who’s gone.”\n\n“That’s not an answer.”\n\n“It’s the only one I have.” She stands, dusting off her knees. “The healers will be here soon. We need to assess the rest of the dome before—”\n\n“Stop.” Jhace pushes himself upright, legs shaking. “Just stop.”\n\nTiffani goes still.\n\n“You’ve been holding back,” Jhace says. “This whole time. You could’ve fixed the dome yourself. You didn’t need me.”\n\n“That’s not—”\n\n“Then why?” His voice is rougher than intended. “Why hire me? Why teach me? Why let me overextend when you could’ve just—”\n\n“Because I can’t do this alone!” Tiffani’s voice cracks—just slightly, but enough. “You think I’m holding back? I’m barely holding together. Every time I use that technique, my ribs feel like they’re splitting open. Every time I push Form past safe limits, I pay for it. And if I try to stabilize an entire dome alone, I’ll collapse just like you would.”\n\nJhace stares at her. “Then why didn’t you tell me?”\n\n“Because you needed to learn control, not just force. And because—” She exhales shakily. “—because I was scared if I showed you how skilled I really was, you’d stop trusting me. You’d think I was using you.”\n\n“Are you?”\n\nTiffani flinches. “No.”\n\n“Then what are you doing?”\n\nShe doesn’t answer immediately. She just stands there, breathing hard, blood on her hands from Kylar’s injury, exhaustion carved into every line of her face.\n\n“I’m trying to survive,” she says finally. “And I need you to survive with me.”\n\nJhace doesn’t know what to say to that. So he just steps closer, close enough that their shoulders touch.\n\n“Next time,” he says quietly, “show me what you can do. All of it. I’d rather know the truth than guess.”\n\nTiffani looks up at him. “Even if the truth is complicated?”\n\n“Especially then.”\n\nShe almost smiles. “You’re stubborn.”\n\n“So are you.”\n\nThey stand in the market square, surrounded by cracked glass and fallen beams, blood on cobblestones and stress-traces glowing faint violet in the early light. The dome holds—barely. Kylar’s alive—barely.\n\nTwo hundred people will trade beneath this structure tomorrow, and Jhace doesn’t know how long it will last.\n\nBut for now, it holds.\n\nThe healers arrive—three of them, carrying tactile boards and bandage rolls. They take over Kylar’s care, murmuring quiet assessments. One healer glances at Jhace’s bleeding hand and gestures for him to sit.\n\nHe does. Too exhausted to argue.\n\nTiffani kneels beside him, watching the healer unwrap his bandages. The tendon splits are worse—fresh tears layered over old scars. The healer shakes her head.\n\n“You need to stop using this hand,” she says bluntly. “Or you’ll lose function permanently.”\n\nJhace doesn’t respond. He just stares at his palm—blood and scar tissue, trembling fingers, veins that glow faint from overuse.\n\nTiffani reaches out, threading her fingers through his left hand. “We’ll figure it out.”\n\n“How?”\n\n“Together.” She squeezes his hand gently. “Same way we’ve figured out everything else.”\n\nJhace closes his eyes, leaning his head back against the dome’s base. He feels her warmth beside him, hears the hum of heat pipes beneath cobblestones, the distant clang of market vendors setting up stalls despite the fracture pattern glowing above them.\n\nThe world is broken. But they’re still here.\n\nThat has to count for something.\n\nThe healer finishes wrapping his hand—fresh bandages, thicker than before. She marks something on her tactile board and leaves.\n\nJhace and Tiffani sit in silence, hands intertwined, watching dawn break over the Dregs.\n\n“We need to find who’s sabotaging the dome,” Tiffani says quietly.\n\n“I know.”\n\n“And we need to finish the repairs before they try again.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“And you need to rest.”\n\nJhace almost laughs. “I know.”\n\nTiffani turns her head, meeting his gaze. “I’m sorry I didn’t tell you. About my skill level.”\n\n“I’m sorry I didn’t ask.” He squeezes her hand. “I just… assumed you were learning too. Figured we were figuring this out together.”\n\n“We are.” Tiffani leans closer, resting her forehead against his shoulder. “But I’ve been doing this longer. And I’ve lost more than I’ve saved. That’s… hard to explain.”\n\nJhace doesn’t push. He just sits there, holding her hand, letting her rest against him.\n\nEventually, the market opens. Vendors spread their goods beneath cracked glass. Children run through the square, laughing. Mrs. Kettner sets out bread loaves. Chloen, red scarf bright against the morning light, weaves between buyers carrying messages.\n\nThe Dregs survive. Because that’s what they do.\n\nJhace and Tiffani stand, dusting off their clothes, and walk back toward the storage alcove. They have work to do—mapping the new sabotage, reinforcing the weakest ribs, teaching other workers how to stabilize structures before the Sanitists shut them down permanently.\n\nBut for now, they rest.\n\nThat night, they collapse in the alcove, too exhausted for words. Tiffani curls against Jhace’s side, her hand resting over his wrapped palm. He feels her breath even out, feels the weight of her trust settling against him.\n\nAnd for the first time since the dome cracked, he lets himself believe they might actually survive this.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 7\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nNew sabotage attempt: Fresh stress-risers patterned into dome overnight, causing cascade failure\n\nJhace overextends: Forces Wholeness into glass, hand seizes, tendon splits worsen (permanent damage escalates)\n\nTiffani’s advanced Form: Locks entire dome structure mid-collapse using techniques Jhace has never seen (skill reveal)\n\nKylar injured: Rigger pinned under beam, punctured lung, stabilized by Tiffani’s precision velding\n\nHeat pipes: Background hum, constant presence, origin uninvestigated\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nDome structure: Partially locked by Tiffani’s Form techniques, stress-traces frozen but not healed\n\nJhace’s right hand: Additional tendon splits, healer warns of permanent function loss if use continues\n\nTrust fracture: Jhace confronts Tiffani about hidden skill level, vulnerability exchanged (partial repair)\n\nSabotage evidence: New patterning confirms coordinated attacks, not random structural failure\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nBody: Jhace—tendon splits worsen, right hand approaching permanent dysfunction, blood loss\n\nBody: Tiffani—rib scar flares severely during advanced Form work, chronic pain visible\n\nBody: Kylar—broken ribs, punctured lung, removed from active repairs (crew resource lost)\n\nSocial: Partnership tension—Jhace questions Tiffani’s motives, trust temporarily strained\n\nTime: Second sabotage forces full dome reassessment, repair timeline extended\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nSecond bell: Glass detonation, sabotage cascade begins\n\nDawn: Market opens beneath frozen but unstable dome\n\nNight: Jhace and Tiffani rest in alcove, exhaustion forces pause\n\nNew Constraint (Persists 2–3 Chapters): Jhace’s right hand is approaching permanent dysfunction—healer explicitly warns further use risks total loss of fine motor control. This forces tactical shift: Jhace must rely more heavily on Tiffani and other workers for delicate repairs, limiting his independent capacity. In Chapter 8 (Storage Room Confession), this constraint pressures their relationship—Jhace must confront his inability to “hold the city alone” and accept collaborative dependency. In Chapter 9 (The Quotas Arrive), the constraint forces Jhace to teach others his techniques, distributing the load across the community. Additionally, Tiffani’s skill reveal creates trust vulnerability—Jhace now knows she’s been holding back, raising questions about her true agenda. This tension surfaces in Chapter 8 (intimate conversation about honesty) and Chapter 9 (Tiffani must prove she’s not manipulating him).\n\nRomance Micro-Act (Cluster 3, Act 1 of 2): Jhace chooses to confront Tiffani (vulnerability through honesty demand). Tiffani chooses to admit her fear of rejection (trust through confession). Both choose physical proximity after conflict—forehead to shoulder, hands intertwined, sleeping together as reconciliation.\n\nCHAPTER 8 — Storage Room Confession\n\nTiffani POV\n\nTiffani wakes with Jhace’s arm across her ribs.\n\nThey’re still in the storage room—narrow space, rust smell, tools scattered around the bedroll. Morning light filters through gaps in the boarded window, casting lines across his face. He’s asleep, finally, after hours of tremor-waking and shallow breath.\n\nHis right hand rests against her stomach, bandaged and still. She can feel the heat of it through her shirt—inflammation, chronic pain that never quite fades.\n\nShe doesn’t move. If she does, he’ll wake, and he needs rest more than she needs to escape this conversation they’re inevitably going to have.\n\nLast night, after the dome stabilized, they stumbled here. Adrenaline and relief turned into need—hands finding skin, mouths claiming survival as much as desire. It was rough, urgent, bodies confirming they were both still alive after glass nearly shattered above two hundred people.\n\nNow, in morning light, it feels different. Quieter. More permanent.\n\nTiffani shifts slightly, trying to ease the pressure on her rib scar without waking him. The movement fails—Jhace’s eyes open, unfocused for a moment, then sharpening as he remembers where they are.\n\n“Morning,” he says, voice rough from sleep.\n\n“Morning.” Tiffani doesn’t pull away. “How’s your hand?”\n\nJhace flexes his fingers—or tries to. The tremor is back, visible even in the dim light. “Functional.”\n\n“That’s not an answer.”\n\n“It’s the only one I have.” He shifts, propping himself on his left elbow to look at her properly. “Where did you learn that technique? The one you used on the dome.”\n\nTiffani’s stomach twists. She knew this was coming. Knew the moment she locked the dome ribs with advanced Form that Jhace would start asking questions she doesn’t want to answer.\n\n“My teacher,” she says carefully. “Marisse. I told you about her.”\n\n“You told me she died. You didn’t tell me she taught you how to freeze an entire structure mid-collapse.” Jhace’s tone isn’t accusing—just curious, with an edge of something sharper. “That’s not standard velding. That’s… something else.”\n\nTiffani sits up, pulling her knees to her chest. The rib scar aches—dull, constant reminder of every time she’s pushed Form past safe limits. She presses her palm against it, breathing shallow.\n\n“It’s called stasis locking,” she says finally. “You thread Form through multiple stress channels simultaneously, creating a geometry matrix that holds everything in suspension. It’s temporary—lasts maybe an hour—but it buys time for permanent repairs.”\n\n“How long does it take to learn?”\n\n“Years. Marisse spent a decade mastering it. She taught me for five years before she died.” Tiffani meets his gaze. “I didn’t show you because it’s dangerous. If you misjudge the load distribution, the entire matrix detonates. And the cost…”\n\nShe trails off, pressing harder against her ribs.\n\nJhace reaches out, covering her hand with his. “Show me.”\n\nTiffani hesitates, then lifts the edge of her shirt. The scar is visible—jagged, old, running along her left ribs. But now, in morning light, Jhace can see the newer damage: faint lines branching from the old scar, like cracks in glass. Vein burns. Permanent marks from yesterday’s stasis lock.\n\n“Every time I use that technique, this gets worse,” Tiffani says quietly. “Marisse had the same marks. By the end, her entire torso looked like shattered glass frozen mid-break.”\n\nJhace stares at the burns, then looks up at her face. “And you still used it.”\n\n“Because two hundred people were standing under that dome.” Tiffani lowers her shirt. “And because you were up there, holding a rib that was already failing. If I hadn’t locked the structure, you’d be dead.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a long moment. Then he asks, “Why didn’t you tell me? About your skill level. About the cost.”\n\nTiffani exhales slowly. “Because I didn’t want you to think I was using you.”\n\n“Are you?”\n\n“No.” She turns to face him fully. “I came to Grindheim tracking rumors of a dual-principle wielder. Someone who could hold contradictory resonances without collapsing. You’re rare, Jhace. Extraordinarily rare. And I needed to confirm your capacity for… work that’s coming.”\n\n“What work?”\n\nTiffani hesitates. This is the edge of the truth she’s been hiding—the pattern she’s been tracking across three cities, the reason she left Strathmoor, the knowledge that the purges and collapses aren’t random.\n\nBut she can’t tell him. Not yet. He’s not ready for what’s coming.\n\n“Survival work,” she says finally. “The Sanitists aren’t just targeting the Dregs. They’re coordinating across cities. Halrick, Odern, Vieln—I’ve tracked patterns. Evictions, fires, collapses. Someone’s organizing this. And when it escalates, we’ll need every skilled craftsperson we can find.”\n\nJhace stares at her. “You’ve been planning for war.”\n\n“I’ve been planning for survival.” Tiffani’s voice hardens slightly. “And I needed a partner who could survive with me. That’s why I found you. That’s why I stayed.”\n\n“And the rest?” Jhace gestures at the space between them—the bedroll, the scattered clothes, the intimacy they built through crisis. “Was that part of the plan?”\n\n“No.” Tiffani’s voice softens. “That was real. All of it.”\n\nJhace doesn’t respond immediately. He just sits there, processing, scarred hands resting on his knees. She can see the calculation in his eyes—weighing her honesty against her secrecy, trust against suspicion.\n\n“I don’t like being studied,” he says finally.\n\n“I know.”\n\n“And I don’t like being lied to.”\n\n“I didn’t lie. I just… didn’t tell you everything.” Tiffani reaches out, touching his hand lightly. “But I’m telling you now. Because you deserve to know who you’re working with.”\n\nJhace looks down at her hand on his. “Who am I working with?”\n\nTiffani exhales, committing. “Someone who lost her teacher to a structural collapse she couldn’t prevent. Someone who’s been tracking patterns across cities, trying to understand why the world is breaking faster than we can fix it. Someone who found you because you’re the only person I’ve met who refuses to let things fall—even when it costs you everything.”\n\nShe pauses, meeting his gaze. “And someone who didn’t expect to care about you. But I do. More than I planned.”\n\nJhace stares at her for a long beat. Then he threads his fingers through hers, gripping tight. “I care about you too. Even when you’re keeping secrets.”\n\n“I’ll try to keep fewer.”\n\n“That’s all I’m asking.”\n\nThey sit in silence, hands intertwined, morning light cutting across scarred skin and old wounds. Outside, the Dregs are waking—bells ringing, vendors setting up stalls, children running through alleys. The world continues, broken but functional.\n\nEventually, Jhace speaks. “We should check the dome. Make sure the stasis lock is holding.”\n\n“It will hold. For another six hours, at least.”\n\n“Then we have time.” He shifts closer, resting his forehead against hers. “Teach me how to do it. The stasis lock.”\n\nTiffani pulls back slightly, frowning. “Jhace, no. That technique will shred your hands. You’re already—”\n\n“I’m already broken.” His voice is flat, matter-of-fact. “But I’m still here. And if there’s a way to hold structures longer, I need to learn it.”\n\n“It’s not sustainable. Marisse died using this technique. I’m damaging myself every time I—”\n\n“Then teach me so I can share the load.” Jhace squeezes her hand. “You said craft is only sustainable when the weight’s shared. So let me carry some of it.”\n\nTiffani stares at him—stubborn, exhausted, refusing to quit even when his body is giving out. She wants to argue. Wants to protect him from the cost she’s already paying.\n\nBut she also knows he’s right. They can’t hold the Dregs alone. Not forever.\n\n“Fine,” she says finally. “But not today. You need rest. And I need to make sure the dome repairs are stable before we start teaching advanced techniques.”\n\nJhace nods, accepting the compromise. “Tonight?”\n\n“Tonight.” Tiffani stands, offering her hand to pull him upright. “But first, we eat. You look like death.”\n\n“Romantic.”\n\nShe almost smiles. “Accurate.”\n\nThey dress quickly, checking tools and supplies. Tiffani wraps Jhace’s hand with fresh bandages, noting the new splits layered over old scars. He’ll lose function eventually. She knows it. He knows it. Neither of them says it aloud.\n\nWhen they emerge from the storage room, Brighlee is waiting at the entrance, tactile board tucked under her arm.\n\n“New postings,” she says without preamble. “You need to see this.”\n\nTiffani and Jhace exchange a glance, then follow Brighlee to the central square. The tactile board is mounted at the district’s edge, ridges freshly carved.\n\nTiffani runs her fingers over the glyphs. Bell mark—two weeks. Quill square—documentation. Ring glyph—civic authority. Stamp bite—penalties for non-compliance.\n\nBut there’s something new. A crossed line beneath the bell mark. Deadline reduced.\n\n“One week,” Brighlee says quietly. “They moved the eviction timeline. If the Dregs don’t meet compliance quotas in seven days, the entire district gets condemned.”\n\nJhace’s jaw tightens. “That’s not enough time.”\n\n“No,” Tiffani agrees. “It’s not. Which means the Sanitists don’t want compliance. They want justification.”\n\nBram approaches from behind—older rigger, scarred hands, pragmatic voice. “They want us to fail. Then they can clear the district with civic approval.”\n\nTiffani stares at the posting, mind racing through calculations. One week. Forty structures marked for repair. Half of them death traps. The other half barely salvageable.\n\nThey can’t save everyone. They never could.\n\n“We need to prioritize,” Tiffani says. “Assess which buildings are worth fighting for, and let the rest go.”\n\nJhace turns to face her. “I don’t let go.”\n\n“I know.” Tiffani’s voice softens. “But if we try to save everything, we’ll save nothing. Including ourselves.”\n\nJhace doesn’t respond. He just stares at the posting, hands trembling, exhaustion and refusal warring in his expression.\n\n“We start tonight,” he says finally. “Mark the priorities. Recruit workers. Distribute the load.”\n\n“Agreed.” Tiffani reaches out, threading her fingers through his. “Together.”\n\nJhace squeezes her hand, then releases it. “I need to find Kylar. See who else we can recruit.”\n\nHe walks away, shoulders tight, already planning the work ahead.\n\nTiffani watches him go, then turns back to the posting. She runs her fingers over the crossed line—deadline reduced. Someone’s accelerating the timeline. Someone wants the Dregs gone before they can organize resistance.\n\nShe pulls the folded map from her pocket—the one she’s been marking for weeks. Burn sites in Halrick. Collapse zones in Mourne. Perfect districts in Vieln. Healing communes in Odern.\n\nA pattern. Coordinated. Escalating.\n\nShe needs to tell Jhace. But not yet. He’s barely holding together. Adding this weight might break him.\n\nSo she folds the map, tucks it away, and follows him into the Dregs.\n\nThe work continues. Because it always does.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 8\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nStasis locking revealed: Tiffani’s advanced Form technique—freezes structures mid-collapse via geometry matrix (temporary, high cost, dangerous)\n\nVein burns visible: New scars branching from Tiffani’s rib injury, permanent marks from stasis lock use\n\nTiffani’s hidden agenda revealed: Came to Grindheim tracking dual-principle wielders, planning for coordinated Sanitist escalation\n\nDeadline reduced: Eviction timeline shortened from two weeks to one week (Brighlee delivers new posting)\n\nHeat pipes: Background hum, no investigation\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nJhace and Tiffani’s partnership: Trust deepened through confession, but tension remains over secrecy\n\nNew eviction posting: One-week deadline, compliance quotas, crossed line glyph (timeline reduction)\n\nTiffani’s tracking map: Marked with burn sites (Halrick), collapse zones (Mourne), perfect districts (Vieln), healing communes (Odern)—pattern evidence hidden from Jhace\n\nStasis lock pedagogy proposed: Jhace asks to learn advanced technique, Tiffani agrees (scheduled for tonight)\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nBody: Tiffani—new vein burns visible, rib scar worsens, chronic pain escalates\n\nBody: Jhace—hand tremor persists, tendon splits not healing, function declining\n\nEmotional: Tiffani confesses hidden agenda, risks trust fracture (partially repaired)\n\nSocial: Deadline reduction creates panic in Dregs, pressure on Jhace/Tiffani to prioritize repairs\n\nTime: One-week deadline creates hard constraint for all future repairs\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nMorning: Jhace and Tiffani wake in storage room, post-intimacy conversation\n\nMidday: Brighlee delivers new posting, deadline revealed\n\nNight (planned): Tiffani begins teaching Jhace stasis locking technique\n\nNew Constraint (Persists 2–3 Chapters): One-week eviction deadline forces brutal triage—Jhace and Tiffani must abandon structures they could’ve saved given more time. This moral pressure surfaces in Chapter 9 (The Quotas Arrive—mapping priorities, recruiting workers, distributing load) and Chapter 10 (Nights Become Strategy—illegal night repairs under surveillance, workers collapsing from exhaustion). Additionally, Tiffani’s confession creates transparency obligation—Jhace now expects honesty, so future secrets (tracking map, pattern recognition) risk partnership collapse. In Chapter 12 (Refugees Tell Stories), this constraint surfaces when Tiffani hears refugee accounts matching her map data and must decide whether to share with Jhace or continue protecting him from knowledge he’s not ready for.\n\nRomance Micro-Act (Cluster 3, Act 2 of 2): Jhace chooses to accept Tiffani’s confession (trust despite secrecy). Tiffani chooses to reveal hidden agenda (vulnerability through honesty). Both choose partnership continuation—hands intertwined, commitment renewed despite tension.\n\nCHAPTER 9 — The Quotas Arrive\n\nJhace POV\n\nThe list is longer than Jhace expected.\n\nHe stands in the central square at dawn, reading Brighlee’s tactile board with fingers that still tremble from yesterday’s dome stabilization. Bell mark—one week. Quill square—documentation. Ring glyph—enforcement. Stamp bite—penalties.\n\nBelow the standard glyphs, a new section: specific addresses. Forty buildings marked for immediate inspection. Thirty-two condemned structures requiring compliance repairs within seven days or forced eviction.\n\nJhace counts the addresses. Half the Dregs.\n\n“It’s impossible,” Kylar says from behind him. The engineer is leaning against the posting board, arms crossed, exhaustion carved into his face. “We can’t repair thirty-two structures in a week. Not with our crew.”\n\n“Then we recruit more workers,” Jhace says.\n\n“From where?” Kylar gestures at the Dregs. “Everyone who can work is already working. The rest are too old, too young, or too sick.”\n\nJhace doesn’t answer. He’s already running calculations—repair timelines, material costs, worker availability. Some structures can be saved with velding and bracing. Others need complete reconstruction. A few are death traps held together by habit and prayer.\n\nTiffani arrives, carrying two cloth-wrapped loaves from Mrs. Kettner’s stall. She hands one to Jhace without speaking, then runs her fingers over the tactile board. Her expression doesn’t change, but Jhace sees the tension in her shoulders.\n\n“We need to prioritize,” she says finally.\n\n“I know.”\n\n“No.” Tiffani turns to face him. “I mean we need to let go. Some of these buildings can’t be saved. Not in seven days. If we try, we’ll exhaust ourselves and save nothing.”\n\nJhace’s jaw tightens. “I don’t abandon people.”\n\n“I’m not asking you to abandon them. I’m asking you to be strategic.” Tiffani pulls a folded paper from her belt—the map she’s been marking for weeks. “We assess the structures, mark the ones worth fighting for, and help the others relocate before the Sanitists force them out violently.”\n\n“Relocate where?” Kylar asks. “The condemned zones don’t have housing. And civic shelters won’t take Dregs residents.”\n\nTiffani doesn’t have an answer for that. None of them do.\n\nBram approaches—older rigger, scarred hands, pragmatic voice. “She’s right, Jhace. You can’t save everyone. You need to choose.”\n\nJhace stares at the tactile board. Thirty-two addresses. Hundreds of families. He knows most of them by name—Mrs. Kettner on the eastern block, Chloen and her mother in the northern tenement, Kylar’s workshop on the southern edge.\n\nGood intentions aren’t an engineering property.\n\nHe learned that lesson five years ago when he forced a bridge repair and killed two workers. Learned it again when the scaffold collapsed and three riggers died. Learned it every time he bled himself empty trying to hold structures that should’ve been abandoned years ago.\n\n“Fine,” Jhace says quietly. “We prioritize.”\n\n\n\nThey spend the morning mapping the district. Tiffani leads, marking structures on her paper map with quick, efficient strokes. Jhace follows, assessing each building’s stability with hands and eyes.\n\nThe eastern tenement—three-degree lean, corroded anchors, but salvageable with bracing and joint reinforcement. Priority one.\n\nThe northern scaffold—failed weld, active vein rupture, but Kylar’s crew uses it daily. Can be stabilized with velding. Priority two.\n\nThe southern warehouse—collapsing roof, rotten joists, no occupants. Condemned. Let it fall.\n\nMrs. Kettner’s building—stairs leaning four degrees, foundation cracked, but she’s lived there sixty years and refuses to leave. Priority one, not because it’s salvageable, but because abandoning her isn’t negotiable.\n\nBy midday, they’ve marked twenty structures as saveable. Twelve as condemned.\n\nTwelve buildings. Thirty families minimum. Maybe fifty.\n\nJhace stares at the map, feeling the weight of those names settle into his chest like stones.\n\n“We’ll help them relocate,” Tiffani says quietly. “Find temporary housing. Negotiate with landlords in adjacent districts.”\n\n“With what?” Jhace asks. “We don’t have coin for bribes.”\n\n“We have labor. And we have leverage.” Tiffani taps the map. “The Sanitists want compliance. If we demonstrate good-faith effort—repair the saveable structures, evacuate the condemned ones voluntarily—they’ll have less justification for violent clearance.”\n\nBram nods. “It’s strategic retreat. Not surrender.”\n\nJhace doesn’t like it. Strategic retreat sounds too much like giving up. But he also knows Tiffani’s right—they can’t hold every line. Sometimes survival means choosing which battles to fight.\n\n“We start tonight,” Jhace says. “Recruit workers. Teach velding basics. Distribute the load.”\n\n“Agreed.” Tiffani rolls the map, tucking it into her belt. “I’ll coordinate crews. You teach technique.”\n\n“Why me?”\n\n“Because they trust you.” Tiffani meets his gaze. “You’ve been holding this district together for years. They’ll listen to you.”\n\nJhace doesn’t feel trustworthy. He feels exhausted, scarred, barely functional. But he nods anyway.\n\n\n\nThat night, they gather in the market square beneath the stabilized dome. Fifteen workers—riggers, masons, laborers, a few apprentices barely old enough to lift tools. Kylar’s crew. Bram’s network. A young woman named Selenthe who learned basic velding from an Outer Crown mentor years ago.\n\nJhace stands at the center, holding a borrowed velding stylus. His right hand is wrapped in fresh bandages. His left trembles visibly.\n\n“Most of you know brasting,” Jhace says. “Force the vein into compliance. Hammer it until it holds.”\n\nThe workers nod. That’s the Underface way—direct, forceful, effective.\n\n“Velding is different,” Jhace continues. “You don’t force. You guide. You find the stress channel and open pathways for it to follow. Slow. Controlled. Like this.”\n\nHe presses the stylus against a cracked stone slab Tiffani set up for demonstration. He finds the vein—faint, barely active—and exhales. The stylus glows amber. The stress redistributes incrementally.\n\nThe crack doesn’t seal. It just… stops spreading.\n\n“That’s it?” one of the riggers asks, skeptical.\n\n“That’s the foundation,” Tiffani says, stepping forward. “Everything else builds from there. Velding takes time. But it’s sustainable. You won’t shred your hands the way brasting does.”\n\nShe glances at Jhace’s bandaged palm. Everyone notices.\n\n“Show us,” Selenthe says quietly.\n\nTiffani kneels beside the slab, pressing her own stylus against the stone. She traces the vein with deliberate slowness, breathing steady. The crack heals—not instantly, not dramatically, just… incrementally mending as stress redistributes into dormant channels.\n\nThe workers watch in silence.\n\n“How long did it take you to learn that?” Kylar asks.\n\n“Years,” Tiffani says. “But you don’t need mastery. You just need the basics. Hold structures long enough for permanent repairs. Buy time.”\n\nShe stands, gesturing at the group. “We have seven days. Twenty buildings to save. We work in pairs—one braster, one velder. The braster holds. The velder guides. Together, we hold.”\n\nThe workers exchange glances. Some skeptical. Some hopeful. All exhausted.\n\nBram steps forward. “I’m in.”\n\nKylar nods. “Same.”\n\nSelenthe pulls a stylus from her belt. “I’ll teach the apprentices.”\n\nOne by one, the workers commit. Not because they believe they’ll succeed. But because they don’t have another option.\n\n\n\nThey work through the night. Jhace moves between crews, teaching technique, correcting mistakes. His hands ache—chronic pain layered over fresh splits—but he doesn’t stop.\n\nEastern tenement: Bram and Selenthe brace the anchor while Kylar welds stress-risers into the foundation joints. Stable by second bell.\n\nNorthern scaffold: Two apprentices hold a failed joint with Wholeness while Tiffani veldes the weld closed. Jhace watches, noting how she adjusts their breath rhythm, teaches them to open instead of push. She’s a better teacher than he is. Patient. Precise. No wasted words.\n\nMrs. Kettner’s stairs: Jhace handles this himself. The structure is too fragile for apprentices. He presses both hands against the support beam, finds the vein, and holds—not brasting, not velding, just raw Wholeness poured into wood that should’ve been replaced a decade ago.\n\nHis right hand seizes halfway through. Tendon splits tearing wider. Pain floods his arm, white-hot and immediate.\n\nHe doesn’t let go.\n\nTiffani appears beside him, stylus glowing. “Let me take some of it.”\n\n“I’ve got it.”\n\n“Jhace.” Her voice is sharp. “You’re shredding your hand. Let me help.”\n\nHe exhales shakily and loosens his grip—not releasing, just… opening. Tiffani threads Form through his Wholeness, redistributing the load between them. The vein settles. The stairs stabilize.\n\nJhace pulls his hands back, breathing hard. Blood soaks through the bandages on his right palm. Fresh splits layered over old scars.\n\nTiffani wraps his hand with clean cloth, her fingers steady and efficient. “You need to stop using this hand.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“Do you?” She ties the bandage tight. “Because if you keep pushing, you’ll lose function permanently. The healer warned you.”\n\n“I heard her.”\n\n“Then why—”\n\n“Because Mrs. Kettner lives here.” Jhace’s voice is flat. “And I’m not letting her stairs collapse while she’s standing on them.”\n\nTiffani stares at him for a long beat. Then she exhales, shoulders sagging. “You’re impossible.”\n\n“So are you.”\n\nShe almost smiles. “Fair.”\n\nThey stand in silence, hands still touching—hers over his, both scarred and trembling. Around them, the Dregs hum with activity. Workers bracing scaffolds. Apprentices learning to veld. The market dome glowing faint violet above, stress-traces frozen by Tiffani’s stasis lock.\n\n“We’re not going to save everyone,” Tiffani says quietly.\n\n“I know.”\n\n“But we’ll save who we can.”\n\nJhace nods. “That has to be enough.”\n\n\n\nBy dawn, they’ve stabilized twelve structures. Eight remain—too damaged, too dangerous, not enough time. Jhace and Tiffani gather the displaced families, helping them pack essentials, negotiating temporary housing with landlords in adjacent districts.\n\nChloen’s mother carries her daughter on one hip, a bundle of clothes on the other. She stops in front of Jhace, eyes red from crying.\n\n“Thank you,” she says quietly.\n\nJhace doesn’t know how to respond. He didn’t save her building. Just helped her leave before the Sanitists forced her out violently.\n\n“Stay safe,” he says finally.\n\nChloen waves—small hand, red scarf bright against the morning light. “Bye, Jhace!”\n\nHe watches them disappear into the crowd, throat tight.\n\nTiffani’s hand finds his. “You did what you could.”\n\n“It wasn’t enough.”\n\n“It never is.” She squeezes his hand gently. “But you’re still here. That counts.”\n\nJhace closes his eyes, leaning his head back. The bells ring seventh hour. The heat pipes hum beneath cobblestones. The market opens, vendors spreading goods beneath cracked glass.\n\nThe Dregs survive. Because they always do.\n\nThat night, Jhace and Tiffani collapse in the storage alcove, too exhausted for words. They lie side by side, hands intertwined, breathing the same air.\n\n“We have six days left,” Tiffani whispers.\n\n“I know.”\n\n“We’ll finish the repairs. And we’ll teach the workers to hold their own corners.”\n\nJhace turns his head, meeting her gaze in the darkness. “You think it’ll be enough?”\n\n“I think we’ll find out.”\n\nShe shifts closer, resting her forehead against his shoulder. He feels her warmth, her breath evening out as exhaustion pulls her under.\n\nJhace stays awake longer, staring at the ceiling, counting names. Families saved. Families displaced. Structures abandoned. Workers learning to veld.\n\nThe math doesn’t balance. It never does.\n\nBut for now, they hold.\n\nAnd tomorrow, they’ll hold again.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 9\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nTactile posting: New addresses listed—forty buildings for inspection, thirty-two condemned (one-week deadline)\n\nTriage strategy implemented: Jhace and Tiffani prioritize twenty structures, abandon twelve (strategic retreat)\n\nVelding pedagogy: Jhace teaches fifteen workers basic technique—“open pathways, don’t force” (communal skill transfer)\n\nShared vein technique: Tiffani threads Form through Jhace’s Wholeness during Mrs. Kettner’s stair repair (partnership under crisis)\n\nHeat pipes: Background hum, bells mark time, no investigation\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nDistrict priority map: Twenty structures marked saveable, twelve condemned, evacuation plans drafted\n\nWorker collective formed: Fifteen trained in basic velding (Bram, Kylar, Selenthe, apprentices)\n\nTwelve buildings abandoned: Thirty families relocated to adjacent districts (voluntary evacuation)\n\nJhace’s right hand: Additional tendon splits, healer’s warning repeated, function declining rapidly\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nBody: Jhace—right hand seizes during repair, fresh blood through bandages, chronic pain worsens\n\nBody: Tiffani—rib scar pain during shared vein technique (chronic strain visible)\n\nSocial: Twelve families displaced—Jhace adds names to failure ledger, guilt accumulates\n\nSocial: Community trust deepens—workers commit despite skepticism (“We’re in”)\n\nTime: One week deadline creates brutal pressure, forces night work, exhaustion compounds\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nDawn: Brighlee delivers new postings, forty buildings marked\n\nMorning to midday: District assessment, priority mapping\n\nNight: Worker training session, fifteen recruits learn velding basics\n\nRepairs through night: Eastern tenement (second bell), northern scaffold, Mrs. Kettner’s stairs\n\nSeventh bell (dawn): Market opens, displaced families relocate\n\nNew Constraint (Persists 2–3 Chapters): Worker collective established—Jhace and Tiffani are no longer sole practitioners holding the Dregs. Fifteen workers now possess basic velding capacity, creating distributed responsibility but also quality control risk (apprentices may misjudge repairs, causing new failures). In Chapter 10 (Nights Become Strategy), this constraint pressures coordination—Jhace must supervise multiple crews simultaneously, preventing solo overextension but introducing communication failures. In Chapter 11 (Sanitist Raid), the constraint becomes vulnerability—Sanitists target trained workers, threatening to dismantle the collective. Additionally, Jhace’s right hand is approaching total dysfunction—continued use risks permanent paralysis. This forces reliance on left-hand work and Tiffani’s assistance, limiting independent capacity further.\n\nRomance Micro-Act (Cluster 3, Act 1 of 2 continued): Jhace chooses to accept Tiffani’s help during Mrs. Kettner’s repair (vulnerability through trust). Tiffani chooses to wrap his hand afterward (care as intimacy). Both choose to rest together—hands intertwined, forehead to shoulder, partnership reaffirmed through exhaustion.\n\nCHAPTER 10 — Nights Become Strategy\n\nTiffani POV\n\nThe crews are failing.\n\nTiffani stands in the central square at dusk, watching three apprentices attempt to brace a scaffold anchor on the southern block. They’ve been working for twenty minutes—too long. The anchor should’ve been stabilized in ten.\n\nShe can see the problem from thirty meters away. They’re pushing Wholeness into the joint without tracing the vein first. Brute force instead of precision. It’s not their fault—Jhace taught them the basics in one night, and they’re applying what they learned. But velding requires time, and time is a luxury they don’t have.\n\nFour days left until the deadline.\n\nTwelve structures stabilized. Eight still condemned. Fifteen workers trained, but half of them overextending, collapsing from exhaustion mid-shift.\n\nThe math doesn’t balance. It never did.\n\nKylar approaches from behind, carrying his tactile board. “Northern crew finished the tenement joists. Stable. But Bram’s team hit a problem on the eastern scaffold—weld failed during redistribution. They need your eyes.”\n\nTiffani nods, already moving. She crosses the square fast, dodging market vendors closing their stalls. The heat pipes hum beneath cobblestones, their rhythm constant. She doesn’t have time to think about how tired she is, how her rib scar aches from yesterday’s stasis lock, how Jhace is somewhere across the district pushing himself past safe limits again.\n\nShe reaches the eastern scaffold and climbs. Bram is kneeling on the platform, hands pressed flat against a failed weld. His face is pale, sweat sheeting down despite the cold.\n\n“Can’t hold it,” he says through gritted teeth. “Vein’s collapsing too fast.”\n\nTiffani kneels beside him, pressing her stylus against the metal. She finds the vein—thrashing, rupturing in real-time. Someone overloaded it. Forced Wholeness into a channel that couldn’t absorb the pressure.\n\n“Who worked this section?” she asks.\n\n“Jhace.” Bram’s voice is strained. “Before he moved to the western block. Said it was stable.”\n\nIt was stable. But someone sabotaged it afterward—fresh stress-risers carved into the joint, designed to look like structural fatigue. Tiffani recognizes the pattern. Same technique as the dome sabotage.\n\nSomeone’s targeting their repairs.\n\n“Let go,” Tiffani says quietly.\n\n“If I do, the whole platform drops.”\n\n“I know. Let go anyway.”\n\nBram stares at her, then releases the weld. The platform shudders. Tiffani catches the vein with Form—not holding, not stabilizing, just freezing it mid-collapse. Stasis lock. Temporary. She has maybe fifteen minutes before the matrix detonates.\n\n“Evacuate the platform,” she says. “Now.”\n\nBram doesn’t argue. He climbs down fast, shouting for the crew below to clear the area. Tiffani stays, stylus glowing bright amber, threads of Form locking the scaffold in geometric suspension.\n\nHer ribs are on fire. The old scar pulls tight, and new vein burns branch outward—faint lines like cracks in glass. She can feel them forming. Every time she uses this technique, the damage spreads.\n\nMarisse had the same marks. By the end, her entire torso looked like shattered glass frozen mid-break.\n\nTiffani breathes through the pain, holding the matrix. She needs to redistribute the load before the freeze fails, but the vein is too damaged. Any attempt to guide it will cause cascade failure.\n\nFootsteps on the ladder. Jhace.\n\nHe climbs onto the platform, sees the frozen scaffold, sees Tiffani kneeling with stylus pressed to metal. His face goes pale.\n\n“You’re using stasis lock,” he says.\n\n“Had to.”\n\n“How long?”\n\n“Twelve minutes left. Maybe less.”\n\nJhace drops beside her, pressing both hands against the metal. “Let me take some of it.”\n\n“You can’t. Your right hand—”\n\n“Is fine.” It’s not. Tiffani can see the tremor, the way his fingers won’t fully close. But he’s already finding the vein, threading Wholeness through her Form, redistributing the load between them.\n\nThe matrix stabilizes. The countdown stops.\n\nThey work in silence—synchronized, practiced, no wasted words. Jhace holds. Tiffani guides. Together, they redistribute stress from the failed weld into dormant channels within the scaffold frame.\n\nIt takes six minutes. When the vein finally locks, they pull back simultaneously, breathing hard.\n\nJhace flexes his right hand. Blood soaks through the bandages.\n\n“You’re bleeding,” Tiffani says.\n\n“I know.”\n\n“You need to stop.”\n\n“I will.” He doesn’t mean it. She knows he doesn’t mean it. “After the deadline.”\n\nTiffani wants to argue. Wants to grab his shoulders and shake him until he understands that four more days of this will shred his hand permanently. But she’s too exhausted, and they don’t have time.\n\n“Sabotage,” she says instead, gesturing at the weld. “Fresh stress-risers. Same pattern as the dome.”\n\nJhace stares at the joint, jaw tight. “Who?”\n\n“Someone with craft training. Someone who knows vein mechanics.” Tiffani pulls a cloth from her belt, wrapping Jhace’s hand with quick, efficient movements. “The Sanitists have practitioners on staff. Or they’re hiring contractors to pattern failures, then blaming us for non-compliance.”\n\n“That’s coordinated.”\n\n“Yes.” Tiffani ties the bandage tight. “Which means this isn’t local enforcement. It’s strategic. Someone wants the Dregs gone.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a long moment. Then he asks, “How do we stop it?”\n\n“We don’t.” Tiffani stands, dusting off her knees. “We finish the repairs, meet the quotas, and force them to find another justification. But we can’t stop the sabotage. We can only respond faster than they can escalate.”\n\nJhace pushes himself upright, legs shaking. “That’s not sustainable.”\n\n“I know.”\n\nThey climb down the scaffold in silence. The crew below has scattered—some watching from safe distance, others already moving to the next site. Bram approaches, expression grim.\n\n“That’s the third sabotage this week,” he says. “We’re not going to make the deadline if they keep patterning failures faster than we repair them.”\n\nTiffani knows he’s right. They’ve stabilized twelve structures, but if the Sanitists sabotage them again, all that work is wasted. And they don’t have resources to guard every repair site simultaneously.\n\n“We prioritize,” Tiffani says. “Focus on the structures with highest occupancy. Let the empty buildings fail.”\n\n“That’s six more families displaced,” Kylar says, stepping forward. “Where do they go?”\n\nTiffani doesn’t have an answer. None of them do.\n\nJhace exhales slowly. “We help them relocate before the Sanitists force them out. Negotiate temporary housing. Give them time to pack.”\n\n“With what leverage?” Bram asks.\n\n“Labor.” Jhace meets Tiffani’s gaze. “We offer repair work to landlords in adjacent districts. Free stabilization in exchange for housing displaced families.”\n\nTiffani processes that. It’s smart. Strategic. But it also means splitting their already-exhausted crews across multiple districts, increasing risk of detection and retaliation.\n\n“It could work,” she says finally. “If we coordinate carefully.”\n\nJhace nods. “Then we start tonight.”\n\n\n\nThat night, they divide the crews. Jhace leads six workers to adjacent districts, negotiating with landlords. Tiffani coordinates the remaining nine, racing to finish priority repairs before the next sabotage attempt.\n\nShe moves between sites like a chess player managing simultaneous games. Northern tenement—apprentices learning to veld stress-risers into foundation joists. Western scaffold—Bram bracing an anchor while Selenthe guides redistribution. Mrs. Kettner’s stairs—two workers holding a support beam with Wholeness while Tiffani threads Form through the joint.\n\nEvery site demands her attention. Every mistake risks collapse. Every delay compounds.\n\nBy third bell, she’s exhausted. Her hands shake. Her ribs ache so badly she can barely breathe. But she doesn’t stop.\n\nAt the northern tenement, one of the apprentices—a young man named Dren—overextends. He floods the joist with Wholeness, trying to force stabilization instead of guiding it. The vein detonates.\n\nTiffani hears the crack from two blocks away. She runs.\n\nBy the time she reaches the site, Dren is on the ground, hands bleeding, breath shallow. The joist he was working on has split completely, and the tenement’s northern wall is leaning three degrees off vertical.\n\nThe other apprentices are panicking, trying to brace the wall before it collapses.\n\n“Stop!” Tiffani shouts. “Don’t touch it!”\n\nThey freeze. She drops beside Dren, checking his hands. Tendon splits. Vein burns. Breath debt so severe he can barely speak.\n\nHe overextended. Shredded his craft channels. He won’t recover for weeks. Maybe months.\n\nTiffani wraps his hands with clean cloth, then stands. The wall is unstable—active collapse, maybe ten minutes before it fails completely. She can lock it with stasis, but that’s just buying time. Someone needs to redistribute the load permanently.\n\nJhace isn’t here. He’s across the district, negotiating housing.\n\nTiffani is alone.\n\nShe presses her stylus against the wall, finds the vein, and opens herself to it. Not stasis lock—that would just delay the problem. She needs to redistribute. Guide the stress into dormant channels within the foundation, buy enough time for a permanent repair crew to arrive.\n\nIt’s delicate work. Precision velding. The kind that takes hours under normal conditions.\n\nShe has ten minutes.\n\nTiffani breathes steady, finding the rhythm. She traces the vein with her stylus, mapping every channel, every pressure gradient. The stress wants to cascade—wants to follow the path of least resistance straight into catastrophic failure.\n\nShe gives it a better option.\n\nSlowly, incrementally, she opens pathways within the foundation. The vein responds—not rushing, not collapsing, just easing into new configurations. The wall stops leaning. The stress redistributes.\n\nFive minutes pass. Seven. Nine.\n\nAt ten minutes, the vein locks. The wall stabilizes.\n\nTiffani pulls the stylus back, breathing hard. Her ribs are screaming. New vein burns branch from the old scar, visible through her shirt. She presses her palm against them, trying to ease the pain.\n\nIt doesn’t help.\n\n“Get a permanent crew here,” she says to the nearest apprentice. “Reinforce this wall before dawn. If it fails again, evacuate the building immediately.”\n\nThe apprentice nods and runs.\n\nTiffani stands there, swaying slightly, exhaustion pulling at her edges. She’s done eleven repairs tonight. Supervised nine crews. Prevented three collapses. And she still has four hours before dawn.\n\nFour days until the deadline.\n\nEight structures left to save.\n\nThe math doesn’t balance.\n\n\n\nShe finds Jhace at fourth bell, standing on a rooftop in the adjacent district. He’s watching the Dregs from a distance—tenements leaning, scaffolds holding, heat pipes glowing faint beneath streets.\n\nTiffani climbs up beside him, sitting on the ledge. They don’t speak. Just breathe the same air, sharing exhaustion.\n\n“Did you find housing?” she asks finally.\n\n“Six families. Temporary. One month maximum.” Jhace flexes his right hand, grimacing. “Three landlords said no. They don’t want Dregs refugees.”\n\n“Predictable.”\n\n“Yeah.” He turns to look at her, and his expression shifts. “You’re bleeding.”\n\nTiffani looks down. Blood soaks through her shirt—not from injury, but from vein burns. The new marks have split the skin, and she didn’t notice until now.\n\n“It’s fine,” she says.\n\n“It’s not.” Jhace reaches out, lifting the edge of her shirt carefully. He stares at the burns—branching lines spreading from the old rib scar like cracks in glass. “You’re using stasis lock too much.”\n\n“I don’t have a choice.”\n\n“You do.” His voice is quiet, controlled. “You could teach me. Distribute the load.”\n\n“Jhace, no. That technique will shred your hands completely. You’re already—”\n\n“Already broken.” He meets her gaze. “But I’m still here. And if there’s a way to hold structures longer, I need to learn it.”\n\nTiffani stares at him. She wants to argue. Wants to protect him from the cost she’s already paying. But she also knows he’s right. They can’t hold the Dregs alone. Not forever.\n\n“After the deadline,” she says finally. “If we survive that long.”\n\nJhace nods, accepting the compromise.\n\nThey sit in silence, watching dawn break over the Dregs. The heat pipes hum. The bells ring fifth hour. Workers emerge from tenements, moving toward market stalls, scaffolds, repair sites.\n\nThe world continues. Broken, but functional.\n\n“We’re going to lose,” Jhace says quietly.\n\n“I know.”\n\n“But we’ll hold as long as we can.”\n\nTiffani reaches out, threading her fingers through his. “That’s the work.”\n\nJhace squeezes her hand gently. “Thank you.”\n\n“For what?”\n\n“For staying. For teaching. For…” He trails off, searching for words. “For not letting me do this alone.”\n\nTiffani leans into him, resting her head against his shoulder. “You’ll never have to again.”\n\nThey sit there until sixth bell, hands intertwined, watching the Dregs wake beneath a sky that promises nothing but more work, more crisis, more impossible choices.\n\nBut for now, they hold.\n\nAnd tomorrow, they’ll hold again.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 10\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nWorker collective exhaustion: Fifteen trained workers, half overextending, quality control issues emerging (Dren overextends, causes collapse)\n\nFresh sabotage pattern: Third sabotage this week, stress-risers patterned into repaired joints (coordinated attacks confirmed)\n\nStasis lock repeated use: Tiffani uses technique twice in one night—eastern scaffold, northern tenement wall (vein burns spread visibly)\n\nJhace’s hand worsens: Blood through bandages during shared vein work, healer’s warning reinforced\n\nHeat pipes: Background hum, bells mark time, no investigation\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nEastern scaffold sabotaged: Fresh stress-risers discovered, stabilized via shared vein technique (Jhace + Tiffani)\n\nNorthern tenement wall: Dren’s overextension causes collapse, Tiffani stabilizes alone via precision velding\n\nDren injured: Tendon splits, vein burns, breath debt—removed from active repairs (crew resource lost)\n\nHousing negotiations: Jhace secures temporary housing for six families (one month maximum)\n\nTiffani’s vein burns: New marks spread from rib scar, bleeding through
