shirt (chronic damage escalates)\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nBody: Jhace—right hand bleeds during shared vein, function declining rapidly\n\nBody: Tiffani—vein burns spread and bleed, rib scar pain severe, exhaustion compounds\n\nBody: Dren—overextends, shreds craft channels, removed from work (weeks/months recovery)\n\nSocial: Three families rejected by landlords—displacement crisis worsens\n\nTime: Four days remain until deadline, eight structures still condemned\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nDusk: Tiffani supervises crews, coordinates multiple sites\n\nEastern scaffold sabotage discovered, stabilized via shared vein\n\nThird bell: Northern tenement collapse, Dren injured\n\nFourth bell: Jhace returns from housing negotiations, rooftop conversation\n\nFifth-sixth bell: Dawn, Jhace and Tiffani watch Dregs wake\n\nNew Constraint (Persists 2–3 Chapters): Sabotage escalation confirmed—Sanitists (or contractors) are actively patterning failures into repaired structures faster than the crews can stabilize them. This creates perpetual repair loop—crews fix structures that fail again within days, exhausting workers without achieving permanent progress. In Chapter 11 (Sanitist Raid), this constraint triggers crisis: Sanitists raid active repair site during sabotage, forcing Jhace/Tiffani to choose between finishing repair or escaping detection. In Chapter 12 (Refugees Tell Stories), Tiffani hears accounts from other cities confirming sabotage is coordinated across regions, not isolated to Grindheim. Additionally, Dren’s injury establishes attrition pressure—trained workers are collapsing faster than new ones can be taught, reducing collective capacity. Tiffani must decide whether to continue training (risking more injuries) or restrict work to experienced practitioners (reducing repair coverage).\n\nRomance Micro-Act (Cluster 4, Act 1 of 2): Jhace chooses to ask for stasis lock training (vulnerability through admission of need). Tiffani chooses to show her bleeding vein burns (trust through physical exposure). Both choose proximity—hands intertwined, head to shoulder, partnership reaffirmed despite exhaustion.\n\nCHAPTER 11 — Sanitist Raid\n\nJhace POV\n\nThe raid comes at midnight.\n\nJhace is balanced on the eastern scaffold, hands pressed flat against a failed anchor bolt, when he hears boots on cobblestone. Too many. Moving too fast. Not patrol—enforcement.\n\n“Sanitists,” Tiffani hisses from below. “At least six.”\n\nJhace doesn’t let go of the bolt. The anchor is half-stabilized—vein held by Wholeness, but not locked. If he releases now, the entire platform collapses, and two workers are still up here with him.\n\n“How long?” Tiffani asks, already moving toward the base of the scaffold.\n\n“Three minutes. Maybe less.”\n\n“We don’t have three minutes.”\n\nJhace knows. The boots are getting closer—organized sweep pattern, not random patrol. Someone reported the illegal repair. Or the Sanitists have been watching, waiting for the right moment to strike.\n\n“Get the workers out,” Jhace says. “I’ll finish this.”\n\n“Jhace—”\n\n“Go!”\n\nTiffani hesitates—one second, maybe two—then climbs fast, reaching the platform. She grabs the nearest worker—Selenthe, holding a support beam with shaking hands—and pulls her toward the ladder.\n\n“Down. Now.”\n\nSelenthe doesn’t argue. She releases the beam and climbs, fast and controlled. The second worker—an apprentice named Marrik—starts to follow.\n\n“Wait,” Jhace says. “Hold the secondary joint. Just for two minutes.”\n\nMarrik stares at him, eyes wide. “I can’t—”\n\n“You can.” Jhace meets his gaze. “Find the vein. Breathe. Open the pathway. Just like Tiffani taught you.”\n\nMarrik swallows hard, then presses his palms against the joint. His hands glow faint amber—Form resonance, basic velding. It’s shaky, unsteady, but it’s there.\n\n“Good,” Jhace says. “Hold it.”\n\nBelow, voices. Sharp. Authoritative.\n\n“Illegal repair site. Identify yourselves.”\n\nTiffani’s voice, calm and measured: “Civic contract. We have documentation.”\n\n“No contracts authorized for this district after seventh bell. You’re in violation.”\n\nJhace doesn’t listen to the rest. He focuses on the anchor bolt, pouring Wholeness into corroded metal that wants to fail. The vein thrashes—stress channels rupturing faster than he can stabilize them.\n\nHis right hand seizes. Tendon splits tearing wider. Pain floods his arm, white-hot and immediate.\n\nHe doesn’t let go.\n\nThe anchor locks. Barely.\n\n“Marrik,” Jhace says quietly. “Release the joint. Climb down. Slow.”\n\nMarrik pulls his hands back, trembling. He climbs down fast—too fast, nearly slips on the third rung. But he reaches ground level.\n\nJhace is alone on the platform.\n\nHe releases the anchor bolt, flexing his right hand. Blood soaks through the bandages. The tremor is back, violent, fingers locking into a claw.\n\nBelow, the Sanitists have surrounded Tiffani and the workers. Six inspectors—white uniforms, enforcement badges, one carrying a tactile ledger. The leader steps forward, scanning the group.\n\n“Names. District of origin. Craft affiliations.”\n\nTiffani doesn’t flinch. “Tiffani Merrow. Strathmoor. Independent contractor.”\n\n“Strathmoor.” The inspector’s tone sharpens. “Outer Crown. What’s an Outer Crown velder doing in the Dregs after curfew?”\n\n“Earning a living.”\n\n“By violating civic ordinances.” The inspector gestures at the scaffold. “This structure was condemned. Unauthorized repairs are criminal activity.”\n\n“The structure was condemned unjustly. We stabilized it to prevent casualties.”\n\n“That’s not your decision to make.”\n\nJhace climbs down the scaffold, slow and deliberate. His hands are shaking so badly he can barely grip the rungs. By the time he reaches ground level, the inspectors have turned toward him.\n\nThe leader—tall, sharp-eyed, enforcement badge marked with ring glyph—steps forward.\n\n“Jhace Torrins. Known agitator. Multiple violations of civic compliance codes.”\n\nJhace doesn’t respond. He just stands there, cradling his right hand against his chest.\n\n“You’re under arrest,” the inspector says. “Illegal craft use. Unauthorized structural alterations. Incitement to civil disobedience.”\n\n“He saved lives,” Tiffani says, voice hard. “That scaffold would’ve collapsed and killed two families.”\n\n“That’s speculation.” The inspector gestures to his team. “Detain them. All of them.”\n\nTwo inspectors move toward Jhace. He doesn’t resist. Doesn’t run. Just lets them bind his wrists with cord—rough, efficient, tight enough to hurt.\n\nTiffani’s wrists are bound next. Then Selenthe. Then Marrik.\n\n“What about the others?” one inspector asks, gesturing at the workers scattered throughout the Dregs.\n\nThe leader scans the district, calculating. “Mark their names. We’ll process them tomorrow. Focus on the primary agitators tonight.”\n\nThey’re led through the Dregs in a line—hands bound, boots echoing on cobblestone. Workers watch from windows, silent. No one intervenes. No one shouts protest.\n\nThe Dregs know better. Resistance just makes the Sanitists escalate.\n\nJhace looks back once, seeing the eastern scaffold standing stable, the anchor bolt locked, the platform holding. Three minutes of work. Permanent cost.\n\nWorth it.\n\nThey’re taken to the civic enforcement hall—stone building, narrow cells, tactile boards mounted at every junction. The inspectors separate them. Jhace is shoved into a cell alone—small space, cold floor, iron door with no window.\n\nHe sits against the wall, cradling his right hand. Blood has soaked through the bandages completely. He can’t feel his fingers anymore. Just… pressure. Pain. Numbness spreading.\n\nHe flexes his left hand. Still functional. For now.\n\nFootsteps outside. The door opens. The lead inspector enters, carrying a tactile ledger.\n\n“Jhace Torrins. Age thirty-two. No civic affiliation. No guild protection.” The inspector runs his fingers over the ledger’s ridges. “You’ve been warned three times. Fined twice. And yet you continue illegal repairs.”\n\n“People need housing,” Jhace says flatly.\n\n“People need to comply with civic standards. Condemned structures are condemned for a reason.”\n\n“Because you want the land. Not because the buildings are unsalvageable.”\n\nThe inspector’s expression doesn’t change. “That’s not your concern.”\n\n“It’s exactly my concern.”\n\nThe inspector closes the ledger. “You’ll be processed tomorrow. Fines. Possibly detention. Depends on your cooperation.”\n\n“Cooperation with what?”\n\n“Information.” The inspector kneels, eye-level. “We know you’ve been organizing illegal repair networks. Training unlicensed workers. Distributing craft techniques without guild authorization. Give us names. Locations. We reduce your charges.”\n\nJhace stares at him. “No.”\n\n“No?”\n\n“I don’t give up people trying to survive.”\n\nThe inspector stands. “Then you’ll face full prosecution. And your partner—Tiffani Merrow—will be deported back to Strathmoor. Outer Crown residents aren’t permitted long-term residence in Underface districts without approved contracts.”\n\nJhace’s stomach twists. “She has a contract.”\n\n“Not anymore. Her permit expired three days ago. She’s here illegally.”\n\nJhace didn’t know that. Tiffani never mentioned permits, expiration dates, deportation risk. She just… stayed. Worked. Held the line beside him.\n\nAnd now the Sanitists have leverage.\n\n“Give us names,” the inspector says. “Or she leaves. Tonight.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a long moment. He thinks about Bram, teaching him to Set properly. Kylar, coordinating crews. Selenthe, learning to veld. Mrs. Kettner, baking bread in a condemned tenement. Chloen, red scarf bright against morning light.\n\nThe Dregs. Surviving because they refuse to quit.\n\n“No,” Jhace says finally.\n\nThe inspector’s jaw tightens. “You’re protecting people who can’t protect you.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“That’s not noble. It’s stupid.”\n\n“Maybe.” Jhace meets his gaze. “But it’s the work.”\n\nThe inspector leaves without another word. The door slams shut. Jhace sits in the dark, listening to footsteps fade, bells ring eighth hour, heat pipes hum beneath stone floors.\n\nHe doesn’t know how long he sits there. Time blurs. His hand throbs—chronic pain layered over fresh splits. He tries to flex his fingers. Three respond. Two don’t.\n\nEventually, the door opens again. Not the inspector. Tiffani.\n\nShe’s escorted by a guard, hands still bound. The guard shoves her into the cell, then locks the door behind her.\n\nTiffani drops beside Jhace, breathing hard. “They’re deporting me.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“You told them no.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\nShe stares at him. “Why?”\n\n“Because I don’t give up people trying to survive.” Jhace shifts slightly, leaning his shoulder against hers. “Even if it costs me everything.”\n\nTiffani exhales shakily. “You’re impossible.”\n\n“So are you.”\n\nShe almost laughs. Then she leans into him, resting her head against his shoulder. “They’re processing us at dawn. Fines. Detention. Deportation.”\n\n“Can we fight it?”\n\n“Not without guild backing. And neither of us has guild protection.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a moment. Then he asks, “What happens to the Dregs?”\n\n“They survive. Like they always do.” Tiffani threads her fingers through his left hand—the functional one. “Bram and Kylar will keep coordinating repairs. Selenthe will teach the apprentices. The work continues.”\n\n“Without us.”\n\n“For now.” She squeezes his hand gently. “But not forever.”\n\nJhace doesn’t know if he believes that. But he holds onto it anyway.\n\nThey sit in the dark, hands intertwined, listening to the heat pipes hum. Outside, the Dregs sleep—or try to. Workers brace scaffolds. Tenants pack essentials, preparing for evictions that might come tomorrow or next week or never.\n\nThe world continues. Broken, but functional.\n\nAt dawn, the door opens. Two guards. No inspector.\n\n“Tiffani Merrow. You’re being escorted to the northern gate. Deportation effective immediately.”\n\nTiffani stands slowly. She looks down at Jhace, and for a moment, her controlled expression cracks. “I’ll come back.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“And when I do, we finish the repairs.”\n\n“Deal.”\n\nShe leans down, pressing her forehead against his. No kiss. Just presence. Then she’s pulled away, hands still bound, boots echoing on stone as the guards lead her out.\n\nJhace sits alone in the cell, watching dawn light filter through a high window. His right hand is numb. His left still trembles.\n\nBut he’s still here.\n\nAnd the work continues.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 11\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nSanitist raid: Six inspectors conduct midnight enforcement sweep, arrest Jhace, Tiffani, Selenthe, Marrik (organized crackdown)\n\nJhace overextends: Holds anchor bolt alone, right hand seizes, tendon splits worsen, fingers lose function (two non-responsive)\n\nInterrogation leverage: Inspector offers reduced charges for names/locations—Jhace refuses\n\nTiffani’s deportation: Permit expired three days ago, Outer Crown residency illegal in Underface without approved contract\n\nHeat pipes: Background hum in enforcement hall, bells mark time\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nEastern scaffold stabilized: Anchor bolt locked, platform holds (repair completed despite raid)\n\nJhace arrested: Bound, detained, faces fines/detention (civic record marked)\n\nTiffani deported: Escorted to northern gate, returned to Strathmoor (partnership physically separated)\n\nSelenthe and Marrik detained: Workers arrested, names recorded (collective targeted)\n\nJhace’s right hand: Two fingers non-responsive, function declining toward permanent paralysis\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nBody: Jhace—right hand loses two-finger function, blood loss, chronic numbness spreading\n\nSocial: Jhace refuses to give names—protects Dregs network but faces full prosecution\n\nSocial: Tiffani deported—partnership separated, repair coordination disrupted\n\nSocial: Workers detained—Selenthe and Marrik removed from active repairs, names recorded for future targeting\n\nTime: Dawn deportation creates immediate separation, repair timeline collapses\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nMidnight: Sanitist raid begins, scaffold repair in progress\n\nEighth bell: Jhace detained in cell, interrogation\n\nDawn: Tiffani deported, Jhace remains in custody\n\nNew Constraint (Persists 2–3 Chapters): Tiffani deported—Jhace and Tiffani are physically separated for the first time since Chapter 2. Jhace must coordinate remaining repairs without her guidance, velding expertise, or stasis locking capacity. This forces him to rely entirely on left-hand work (right hand approaching total dysfunction) and delegate advanced techniques to undertrained workers (Bram, Kylar, Selenthe). In Chapter 12 (Refugees Tell Stories—now Jhace solo POV), this constraint surfaces when he attempts dome repair alone and nearly collapses the structure. In Chapter 13 (The Dome Holds), Tiffani returns with new intelligence from Strathmoor, but their reunion is complicated by mutual guilt—Jhace for costing her deportation, Tiffani for leaving him vulnerable. Additionally, Sanitist enforcement has escalated—arrests now target trained workers, dismantling the collective. This creates attrition cascade—every raid removes capacity faster than it can be replaced.\n\nRomance Micro-Act (Cluster 4, Act 2 of 2): Jhace chooses to refuse interrogator’s leverage (protects Tiffani despite cost). Tiffani chooses to promise return (commitment despite separation). Both choose forehead-to-forehead contact before separation (intimacy as reassurance, not goodbye).\n\nINTERLUDE 2A — Mobel (Purity Vessel)\n\n800 words / Third-person limited / City of Halrick\n\nMobel burns his first district at dawn.\n\nHe stands at the edge of the condemned quarter—tenements leaning, streets narrow, heat pipes glowing faint beneath cobblestones. The scent is wrong. Rot. Sweat. Poverty layered over years.\n\nContamination.\n\nHe’s been junior Sanitist enforcer for six months. Issued quotas, filed reports, marked violations on tactile boards. But enforcement isn’t enough. The contamination spreads faster than citations can contain it.\n\nHe tried explaining this to his supervisor. Incremental purging doesn’t work. The filth regenerates.\n\nHis supervisor warned him—You’re not authorized for categorical clearance.\n\nMobel doesn’t need authorization. He needs fire.\n\nHe carries a torch—cloth-wrapped, oil-soaked, lit from the civic hall’s ceremonial brazier. Sacred flame. Purification flame.\n\nBehind him, twelve followers. Not Sanitists. Civilians. Mothers. Merchants. People who’ve listened to his sermons at the civic square.\n\nAddiction is a covenant with filth. Cleanliness is survival. Mercy prolongs suffering.\n\nThey believe him. They should. He’s right.\n\nMobel steps forward, holding the torch high. The tenement district is empty—most residents fled after last week’s eviction notices. A few stubborn stragglers remain, but they’ll evacuate once the fire starts.\n\nHe hopes.\n\nOne of his followers—a woman named Corryn, middle-aged, scarred hands from textile work—steps beside him. “Are we sure this is necessary?”\n\nMobel turns to face her. “Do you believe contamination spreads?”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Do you believe incremental measures fail?”\n\n“…Yes.”\n\n“Then this is necessary.” Mobel’s voice is calm, certain. “We cleanse. Not for cruelty. For survival.”\n\nCorryn nods slowly. She doesn’t look convinced. But she doesn’t leave either.\n\nMobel tosses the torch.\n\nIt arcs through the air, lands on a tenement roof. The thatch catches immediately—dry, brittle, built decades ago without fire-retardant treatment. Flames bloom.\n\nThe district burns.\n\nNot violently. Not explosively. Just… steadily. The fire spreads from roof to roof, consuming wood and tar and accumulated neglect. Smoke rises—black, thick, acrid.\n\nMobel watches. He doesn’t feel satisfaction. Doesn’t feel guilt. Just… relief.\n\nThe contamination is ending.\n\nBehind him, his followers stand in silence. Some weep. Some pray. One vomits.\n\nMobel doesn’t judge them. Purification is hard. Necessary, but hard.\n\nA man stumbles from one of the burning tenements—older, sixty at least, coughing from smoke inhalation. He collapses on cobblestones, staring at Mobel with wide, uncomprehending eyes.\n\n“Why?” the man rasps.\n\nMobel kneels beside him. “Because you refused to leave. Because this district was condemned. Because contamination spreads.”\n\n“We had nowhere to go.”\n\n“That’s not my responsibility.” Mobel’s voice is flat. “My responsibility is cleanliness. You chose to stay in filth.”\n\nThe man doesn’t respond. Just coughs, shaking.\n\nMobel stands, gesturing to Corryn. “Get him to the civic healers. If he survives, he’ll be processed for relocation.”\n\nCorryn drags the man away from the fire. The others watch, unsure whether to help or flee.\n\nMobel turns back to the flames. The district is collapsing now—walls crumbling, roofs caving, heat pipes rupturing beneath streets. The fire doesn’t spread beyond the condemned zone. Mobel planned that carefully. Firebreaks. Wind direction. Controlled burn.\n\nPurification, not chaos.\n\nBy midday, the district is ash.\n\nMobel stands at the edge, staring at the ruins. His followers have scattered—some to report to civic authorities, others to return home, guilt-heavy and silent.\n\nCorryn approaches from behind. “The healers couldn’t save him. The old man. He died from smoke inhalation.”\n\nMobel nods. “Noted.”\n\n“You don’t care?”\n\n“I care.” Mobel turns to face her. “But caring doesn’t change the necessity. He refused to evacuate. That choice has consequences.”\n\nCorryn stares at him. “You sound like you’re reading a civic posting.”\n\n“I am.” Mobel gestures at the ruins. “This is enforcement. Cleaned. Complete.”\n\n“And tomorrow?”\n\n“Tomorrow, we identify the next contamination zone.” Mobel starts walking toward the civic hall. “And we cleanse it.”\n\nCorryn doesn’t follow immediately. She just stands there, staring at the ash.\n\nEventually, she walks away. Not toward the civic hall. Toward her home.\n\nMobel notes her departure. She won’t return. Fear has replaced conviction.\n\nHe doesn’t blame her. Purification requires strength most people don’t have.\n\nBut he does.\n\n\n\nThree weeks later, Mobel opens a school.\n\nIt’s small—stone building, single room, twelve students. Children. Ages six to fourteen. Sent by parents who believe cleanliness is survival.\n\nMobel teaches them purification law. How to identify contamination. How to report violations. How to recognize mercy-through-fire.\n\nThe children listen. They’re good students. Eager. Precise.\n\nOne girl—Lysenne, eight years old, bright-eyed—raises her hand.\n\n“Teacher Mobel. My neighbor. She has tremor-hands. Is that contamination?”\n\nMobel kneels, eye-level. “Does she use craft?”\n\n“Yes. She repairs stairs.”\n\n“Then yes. Craft addiction is contamination.”\n\nLysenne’s face tightens. “Should I report her?”\n\nMobel hesitates. Not because he’s uncertain. Because this is the moment—the first time a child must choose duty over affection.\n\n“Do you believe contamination spreads?” he asks.\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Then report her.”\n\nLysenne nods slowly. She doesn’t look happy. But she looks certain.\n\nMobel stands, addressing the class. “Mercy prolongs suffering. Cleanliness is survival. Purity is duty.”\n\nThe children repeat it. Not loud. Not enthusiastic. Just… steady.\n\nMobel feels relief.\n\nThe doctrine is spreading. Not through force. Through education.\n\nBy year’s end, his school has thirty students. Fifty. A hundred.\n\nParents bring their children to learn purification law. Civic authorities cite his work as model enforcement. Other districts request his guidance.\n\nMobel doesn’t refuse. He teaches. He cleanses. He builds.\n\nHe doesn’t know why this matters so much. Why contamination feels like pain instead of just civic failure. Why fire feels like breathing instead of destruction.\n\nHe just knows he can’t stop.\n\nBehind him, the burned district remains empty. No reconstruction. No replanting. Just ash and scorched stone.\n\nMobel stands at the edge, watching sunset light catch the ruins.\n\n“Next week,” he whispers to no one. “We cleanse the eastern quarter.”\n\nAnd he does.\n\n\n\nINTERLUDE 2B — Eries (Entropy Vessel)\n\n800 words / Third-person limited / City of Mourne\n\nEries stands on a balcony, watching three tenements collapse without touching them.\n\nShe doesn’t push. Doesn’t force. Doesn’t inject Entropy into corroded joints or failing anchors.\n\nShe just… allows.\n\nThe tenements are condemned—scheduled for demolition, residents evacuated, civic contracts issued. But demolitions contractors are brutal. They brast the structures down with force, creating debris clouds, injuring workers, leaving scars on the district.\n\nEries offers a different method.\n\nShe stands on the balcony at dawn, palms resting on the rail, and opens herself to the veins. Not to manipulate them. Just to feel them. The stress channels running through each tenement—ancient, exhausted, wanting to fail.\n\nShe doesn’t stop them.\n\nThe first building sighs. That’s the only word for it. A long, low exhalation of stone and wood surrendering to gravity. The walls don’t shatter. They crumble—slowly, incrementally, dust instead of debris. The structure folds inward, collapsing into its own foundation.\n\nThe second building follows. Then the third.\n\nBy the time the sun clears the horizon, all three tenements are dust.\n\nNo debris field. No injury. Just… release.\n\nEries exhales slowly, lowering her hands. Her ribs ache—dull, chronic pain that never fades. Every time she allows collapse, she feels the structure’s exhaustion in her own body. Weight surrendered. Pressure eased.\n\nIt hurts. But it also feels… right.\n\nBehind her, voices. A small crowd has gathered—residents from adjacent buildings, watching the demolition with silent awe.\n\nOne woman steps forward—Selenthe, mason, scarred hands wrapped in cloth. “How did you do that?”\n\nEries turns to face her. “I didn’t do anything. The buildings wanted to fall. I just… let them.”\n\n“That’s not how demolitions work.”\n\n“No.” Eries smiles—tired, gentle. “It’s not.”\n\nSelenthe stares at the dust settling where three tenements stood moments ago. “You gave them mercy.”\n\nEries doesn’t contradict her. Mercy is the wrong word—but it’s close enough.\n\n“They were suffering,” Eries says quietly. “Held together by habit, not structure. Forcing them to stand longer would’ve been cruelty.”\n\nSelenthe nods slowly. “Can you teach me?”\n\nEries hesitates. She’s never taught anyone. Doesn’t know if what she does can be taught. It’s not technique. It’s… permission. Allowing things to end when they’re ready.\n\n“I can try,” Eries says finally.\n\n\n\nThree weeks later, Eries has twelve students.\n\nThey meet in an abandoned warehouse at the district’s edge. No civic approval. No guild authorization. Just… people who’ve watched structures suffer and want to learn how to release them.\n\nEries doesn’t lecture. She demonstrates.\n\nShe brings them to a condemned scaffold—corroded joints, failing anchors, scheduled for violent demolition. She stands beside it, hands resting lightly on the frame, and listens.\n\nThe scaffold wants to fall. She can feel it—stress channels rupturing in slow motion, veins thrashing, metal screaming silently for release.\n\n“Don’t force it,” Eries says quietly. “Don’t hold it. Just… allow.”\n\nShe exhales, and the scaffold sighs. The joints loosen. The frame settles. The structure collapses gently into the courtyard, folding inward without scattering debris.\n\nHer students watch in silence.\n\nOne apprentice—young man named Corvin, barely twenty—steps forward. “You’re not using Entropy. Not the way contractors do.”\n\n“No,” Eries agrees.\n\n“Then what are you using?”\n\nEries doesn’t know how to answer. She’s not using energy. Not injecting force. She’s just… opening pathways for release. Letting structures end when they’re ready.\n\n“Permission,” she says finally. “I give them permission to let go.”\n\nCorvin stares at her. “That’s not craft.”\n\n“Maybe not.” Eries flexes her fingers, feeling the ache in her ribs. “But it works.”\n\n\n\nBy year’s end, Eries’s following has grown.\n\nFifty students. A hundred. People from across Mourne who’ve heard rumors of “the woman who lets things end.”\n\nThey come to her with requests. Not just structures. Relationships. Debts. Obligations. They want permission to let go of burdens they’ve carried too long.\n\nEries doesn’t refuse. She listens. She acknowledges. She allows.\n\nA woman asks for permission to leave her abusive marriage. Eries says, “You don’t need my permission. But if it helps—yes. Let go.”\n\nA man asks for permission to abandon his family’s failing business. Eries says, “Forcing it to survive prolongs suffering. Let it end.”\n\nA child asks for permission to stop trying to save her dying mother. Eries kneels, eye-level. “Fighting entropy prolongs pain. Mercy is release.”\n\nThe child weeps. But she also… eases.\n\nEries doesn’t know if this is right. Doesn’t know if she’s helping or enabling surrender. But she feels their relief—weight lifted, pressure released, burdens allowed to end.\n\nIt feels… necessary.\n\n\n\nOne night, a civic inspector arrives.\n\nHe’s older, enforcement badge marked with ring glyph, carrying a tactile ledger. He watches Eries teach her students how to release a failing foundation without shattering adjacent structures.\n\nWhen the lesson ends, he approaches.\n\n“You’re teaching people to let things collapse,” he says flatly.\n\n“I’m teaching people to recognize when collapse is inevitable,” Eries corrects. “And how to ease the transition instead of fighting it.”\n\n“That’s not civic doctrine.”\n\n“No. It’s mercy.”\n\nThe inspector is quiet for a moment. Then he says, “The Sanitists are escalating purges across cities. They’re forcing structures to fall violently. Your method… undermines their justification.”\n\nEries meets his gaze. “Good.”\n\nThe inspector almost smiles. “You’re going to attract attention. Dangerous attention.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“And you’ll continue anyway.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\nThe inspector closes his ledger. “I’ll delay my report. Give you time to prepare.”\n\n“Why?”\n\n“Because I’ve seen what violent demolitions do to communities.” His voice softens slightly. “And I’ve seen what your method does. The difference is… undeniable.”\n\nHe leaves without another word.\n\nEries stands in the empty warehouse, feeling the ache in her ribs spread through her chest. Every structure she allows to fall, she carries briefly. Every burden she releases, she feels.\n\nIt’s exhausting.\n\nBut it’s also… right.\n\nShe doesn’t know why endings matter so much. Why collapse feels like mercy instead of failure. Why permission to let go feels like breathing.\n\nShe only knows she can’t stop.\n\nOutside, the city hums. Structures lean. Anchors corrode. And Eries waits for the next thing ready to end.\n\nShe’ll be there when it does.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — INTERLUDE 2A & 2B\n\nNarrative Function: Interludes 2A (Mobel/Purity) and 2B (Eries/Entropy) reveal the second pair of emerging Vessels—ideological extremes that frame the A-plot’s moral terrain. Mobel demonstrates Purity as categorical cleansing (fire as duty, mercy as weakness), while Eries shows Entropy as compassionate release (collapse as mercy, resistance as cruelty). Both Vessels exhibit craft beyond normal limits and unknowingly spread doctrines that will destabilize cities, neither recognizing they are becoming divine anchors. These figures foreshadow the ideological conflicts Jhace and Tiffani will face in N2-N3.\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nPurity resonance: Mobel burns condemned district using fire as purification tool, violence as doctrine (categorical cleansing)\n\nEntropy permission: Eries collapses tenements through “allowing” rather than forcing, release as mercy\n\nCraft cost inversion: Both Vessels experience reduced immediate cost but accumulating moral weight (warning sign of divine emergence)\n\nInnocent framing: Neither character knows they are gods; they believe they are exceptional practitioners serving necessary functions\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nHalrick burned district: Mobel’s first categorical purge, ash zone left empty (no reconstruction)\n\nMobel’s purification school: Twelve students initially, growing to one hundred by year’s end (doctrine spreads through children)\n\nMourne collapsed tenements: Eries’s “gentle demolition” method demonstrated, dust instead of debris\n\nEries’s following: Fifty students initially, growing to one hundred (permission-to-release doctrine spreads)\n\nThematic Pressure on A-Plot (Chapters 12-15):\n\nJhace’s struggle to save structures (Ch9-11) is contextualized—forcing holds prolongs suffering (Eries’s doctrine) vs. abandoning them is failure (Jhace’s guilt)\n\nSanitist persecution (Ch11 raid) connects to Mobel’s doctrine—cleansing as duty, contamination as contagion, enforcement as purification\n\nTiffani’s strategic retreat (Ch14) will echo Eries’s philosophy—knowing when to let go vs. Jhace’s refusal to abandon\n\nForeshadows N2-N3 conflicts: Mobel’s schools spread Sanitist ideology; Eries’s followers enable civic collapses; both destabilize regions\n\nSwitch Flipped (Required for Interludes): Within 1-2 chapters (Ch12-13), refugee accounts reach Grindheim: Jhace hears stories of “fire purges” (Halrick) and “mercy collapses” (Mourne) from displaced families. Tiffani recognizes these match her tracking map patterns (burn sites, collapse zones), confirming the purges are coordinated and ideological, not random civic failures. This shifts her hidden agenda from “study Jhace’s capacity” to “prepare for ideological war”—she begins teaching Jhace advanced techniques not just for survival, but for resistance against spreading doctrines. In Ch13 (The Dome Holds), this surfaces when Tiffani reveals she’s been tracking four cities (Halrick, Mourne, Vieln, Odern) and asks Jhace: “What do you do when the city doesn’t just break—it chooses to?”\n\nNo Continuity Ledger for Interludes (per instructions): Interludes do not receive standard ledger format—they are brief, atmospheric, and exist outside the Jhace/Tiffani timeline. Their function is break-down (introduce forces that will pressure the A-plot through ideological conflict) without direct A-plot interaction yet.\n\nCHAPTER 12 — Refugees Tell Stories\n\nTiffani POV\n\nThe refugees arrive at dawn.\n\nTiffani is coordinating morning repairs—Bram’s crew on the eastern scaffold, Kylar supervising apprentices at the northern tenement—when she sees them: twelve families, carrying what they can, walking through the Dregs with the slow, exhausted gait of people who’ve traveled too far on too little food.\n\nThey’re not local. Their clothes are wrong—too clean for condemned districts, but scorched at the edges. Burn marks. Ash stains.\n\nTiffani stops mid-instruction, watching them approach the central square. Brighlee emerges from the civic office, tactile board tucked under her arm. She speaks briefly with the lead family—older man, wife beside him, three children—then gestures toward the communal well.\n\nTiffani follows.\n\nBy the time she reaches the square, the refugees have collapsed on cobblestones. The children drink from the well, desperate and shaking. The adults sit in silence, staring at nothing.\n\n“Where are they from?” Tiffani asks Brighlee quietly.\n\n“Halrick.” Brighlee’s voice is flat. “Eastern district. Burned three weeks ago.”\n\nTiffani’s stomach twists. Halrick. That’s one of the cities on her tracking map—marked with burn sites, escalating purges, civic clearances justified as cleanliness enforcement.\n\n“Who burned it?” she asks.\n\n“Sanitists. Officially.” The older man speaks without looking up. His voice is hoarse, smoke-damaged. “Unofficially, followers of a preacher named Mobel. Calls himself an enforcer. Teaches children to identify contamination.”\n\nTiffani kneels beside him. “What kind of contamination?”\n\n“Anyone who uses craft. Anyone who refuses to evacuate condemned zones. Anyone who questions civic law.” The man coughs—dry, rattling. “He burned our district at dawn. Said fire cleanses. Said mercy prolongs suffering.”\n\nTiffani feels cold settle into her chest. Fire cleanses. Mercy prolongs suffering. That’s ideology, not enforcement. Someone’s spreading doctrine.\n\n“How many districts?” she asks.\n\n“Four that I know of. Halrick’s eastern quarter. The southern tenements. Two adjacent blocks near the civic hall.” The man meets her gaze. “He’s opening schools. Teaching children purification law. My daughter—seven years old—reported our neighbor for craft addiction. The Sanitists arrested him. He died in detention.”\n\nTiffani doesn’t respond. She just stands, turning to Brighlee. “Are there others? More refugees?”\n\nBrighlee nods. “Three families arrived yesterday. From Mourne. They’re camped in the western alcoves.”\n\nMourne. Another city on Tiffani’s map—marked with collapse zones, accelerated structural failures, civic demolitions justified as safety enforcement.\n\nTiffani walks fast toward the western alcoves, mind racing. If refugees are fleeing Halrick and Mourne, the pattern isn’t isolated. It’s spreading.\n\nShe finds the Mourne families huddled in a narrow alley—two women, five children, one elderly man. They look exhausted but not burned. Just… empty.\n\n“What happened in Mourne?” Tiffani asks without preamble.\n\nOne of the women looks up—middle-aged, scarred hands, eyes red from crying. “The tenements collapsed. All of them. Three districts. Overnight.”\n\n“Structural failure?”\n\n“No.” The woman’s voice is hollow. “Someone… allowed them to fall. A contractor named Eries. She stood on a balcony and the buildings just… sighed. That’s the only word for it. They didn’t shatter. They folded inward. Dust instead of debris.”\n\nTiffani’s pulse quickens. “She used Entropy?”\n\n“Not like contractors use it. No force. No detonation. She just… let them go.” The woman wraps her arms around herself. “Her followers call it mercy. They say she gives structures permission to end when they’re ready.”\n\nPermission to end. Mercy through release.\n\nThat’s not enforcement. That’s philosophy.\n\nTiffani pulls the folded map from her belt, spreading it on cobblestones. Four cities marked—Halrick, Mourne, Vieln, Odern. Burn sites. Collapse zones. Perfect districts. Healing communes.\n\nThe refugees stare at the map.\n\n“What is this?” the woman from Mourne asks.\n\n“A pattern.” Tiffani traces the marked cities with her finger. “Halrick—fire purges. Mourne—mercy collapses. Vieln—I’ve heard rumors of perfect districts where workers faint from exhaustion. Odern—healing communes where patients speak in plural.”\n\nThe elderly man leans forward. “You’re tracking them.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Why?”\n\nTiffani doesn’t answer immediately. She’s been tracking this pattern for months—since before she found Jhace, since before Grindheim. She came here because the rumors suggested a dual-principle wielder, someone rare enough to survive what’s coming.\n\nBut she never told Jhace why she needed him. Never explained that the purges and collapses aren’t random civic failures—they’re coordinated. Ideological. Someone’s spreading doctrines across cities, and those doctrines are destabilizing regions faster than craftspeople can repair them.\n\n“Because someone’s organizing this,” Tiffani says finally. “And I need to understand who, and why, before it reaches Grindheim.”\n\n“It’s already here,” the woman says quietly. “The Sanitists. The evictions. The quotas. That’s how it starts.”\n\nTiffani stares at the map, counting cities. Four confirmed. How many others? How many districts burned before anyone noticed the pattern?\n\nShe folds the map, tucking it away. “How long until the Sanitists escalate?”\n\n“Weeks,” the elderly man says. “Maybe days. They’ll start with arrests. Then detention. Then clearance. By the time you realize it’s coordinated, it’s too late to resist.”\n\nTiffani’s jaw tightens. “Not if we prepare.”\n\n“Prepare how?”\n\n“Distribute the load. Teach workers to hold their own corners. Make it harder to dismantle us all at once.” She stands, dusting off her knees. “If we’re scattered, we’re vulnerable. If we’re organized, we’re a threat.”\n\nThe woman almost smiles. “You sound like someone planning a war.”\n\n“I’m planning survival.” Tiffani meets her gaze. “Same thing.”\n\n\n\nThat night, Tiffani sits in the storage alcove, map spread before her. Jhace is asleep beside her—collapsed after a twelve-hour repair shift, hand wrapped in fresh bandages, breath shallow from chronic exhaustion.\n\nShe doesn’t wake him. He’s been working past safe limits for weeks, and she’s been letting him because stopping means admitting they can’t win.\n\nBut the refugee accounts change the math.\n\nFire purges in Halrick. Mercy collapses in Mourne. Perfect districts in Vieln. Healing communes in Odern.\n\nFour doctrines. Four cities. Four figures spreading ideologies that feel like craft, but aren’t. Something else. Something bigger.\n\nTiffani traces the burn sites on her map. She should tell Jhace. Should explain that the work they’re doing—bracing scaffolds, stabilizing domes, teaching apprentices—is tactical, not just survival. That they’re buying time before something worse arrives.\n\nBut he’s not ready. He’s barely holding together. Adding this weight might break him.\n\nSo she folds the map, hides it beneath the bedroll, and lies down beside him. His hand finds hers in the dark—unconscious gesture, automatic trust.\n\nShe threads her fingers through his, feeling the tremor even in sleep.\n\n“I’ll tell you,” she whispers. “When you’re ready.”\n\nJhace doesn’t respond. Just breathes, steady and exhausted.\n\nTiffani closes her eyes, but sleep doesn’t come. She thinks about Mobel’s sermons. Eries’s permission-to-release. Corlexi’s perfect geometry. Salee’s binding rituals.\n\nPatterns within patterns. Ideologies spreading like contagion.\n\nAnd Jhace—rare, stubborn, dual-principle wielder—caught in the center of something he doesn’t understand yet.\n\nShe needs to tell him. Soon.\n\nBut not tonight.\n\nTonight, she just holds his hand and listens to the heat pipes hum, the bells ring seventh hour, the refugees settling into alcoves across the Dregs.\n\nThe world is breaking faster than they can fix it.\n\nBut for now, they hold.\n\n\n\nThe next morning, Tiffani finds Bram at the eastern scaffold. He’s supervising two apprentices—teaching them to brace anchor bolts without overextending.\n\n“Can we talk?” Tiffani asks.\n\nBram nods, stepping away from the crew. “About the refugees?”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Brighlee told me. Halrick. Mourne.” His expression is grim. “You think it’s coordinated.”\n\n“I know it is.” Tiffani pulls the map from her belt, showing him the marked cities. “Four cities. Four doctrines. Fire, collapse, perfection, binding. All escalating simultaneously.”\n\nBram stares at the map. “That’s not civic enforcement. That’s… strategy.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Who’s behind it?”\n\n“I don’t know. But whoever they are, they’re spreading ideology faster than we can counter it.” Tiffani folds the map. “We need to prepare. Distribute techniques. Teach workers to recognize sabotage patterns. Build networks across districts so if one falls, the others hold.”\n\nBram is quiet for a long moment. Then he says, “You’re talking about resistance.”\n\n“I’m talking about survival.”\n\n“Same thing.” He almost smiles. “Does Jhace know?”\n\n“Not yet.”\n\n“Why not?”\n\n“Because he’ll try to save everyone. And he’ll shred himself doing it.” Tiffani meets Bram’s gaze. “I need him functional. Not martyred.”\n\nBram nods slowly. “Fair. But you’ll have to tell him eventually.”\n\n“I know. Just… not yet.”\n\n“When?”\n\n“When he’s strong enough to carry it.”\n\nBram doesn’t argue. He just gestures at the scaffold. “I’ll start distributing sabotage recognition training. Teach the crews to identify patterned stress-risers. If the Sanitists escalate, we’ll see it coming.”\n\n“Thank you.”\n\nBram turns to leave, then pauses. “Tiffani. If this is coordinated—if someone’s organizing purges across cities—they’ll come for us eventually.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“And when they do, we might not survive.”\n\nTiffani flexes her fingers, feeling the rib scar pull tight beneath her shirt. “Then we hold long enough for someone else to pick up the work.”\n\nBram almost smiles. “You sound like Jhace.”\n\n“Good. He’s right about that part.”\n\n\n\nThat night, Jhace wakes to find Tiffani sitting on the roof, staring at the Dregs. The heat pipes glow faint beneath streets. The bells ring eighth hour.\n\nHe climbs up beside her, sitting close enough that their shoulders touch.\n\n“Couldn’t sleep?” he asks.\n\n“Thinking.”\n\n“About?”\n\nTiffani hesitates. Then she says, “The refugees. The ones from Halrick and Mourne.”\n\n“What about them?”\n\n“They’re not random. The districts they fled—they match patterns I’ve been tracking for months.”\n\nJhace turns to look at her. “What patterns?”\n\nTiffani pulls the map from her belt, spreading it on the roof. “Four cities. Burn sites. Collapse zones. Perfect districts. Healing communes. All escalating simultaneously.”\n\nJhace stares at the map, processing. “You’ve been tracking this since before Grindheim.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Why didn’t you tell me?”\n\n“Because I didn’t want you to carry it until you were ready.” Tiffani meets his gaze. “And because I wasn’t sure what it meant. But the refugee accounts—fire purges, mercy collapses—confirm it. This isn’t civic enforcement. It’s ideology. Someone’s spreading doctrines across cities.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a long moment. Then he asks, “What do we do?”\n\n“We prepare. Distribute techniques. Build networks. Recognize sabotage before it escalates.” Tiffani folds the map. “And we survive long enough to figure out who’s behind it.”\n\nJhace reaches out, threading his fingers through hers. “We do this together.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\nHe squeezes her hand gently. “Thank you. For telling me.”\n\n“I should’ve told you sooner.”\n\n“Maybe.” Jhace leans his head against hers. “But you’re telling me now. That counts.”\n\nThey sit in silence, hands intertwined, watching the Dregs wake beneath a sky that promises nothing but more work, more crisis, more impossible choices.\n\nBut for now, they hold.\n\nAnd tomorrow, they’ll hold again.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 12\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nRefugee arrival: Twelve families from Halrick (fire purges), three families from Mourne (mercy collapses) seek shelter in Dregs\n\nMobel’s doctrine confirmed: Fire cleanses, mercy prolongs suffering—sermons, schools, children reporting neighbors\n\nEries’s doctrine confirmed: Permission-to-release, mercy through collapse—structures “sigh” into dust\n\nTiffani’s tracking map revealed to Jhace: Four cities marked (Halrick, Mourne, Vieln, Odern), pattern confirmed coordinated\n\nHeat pipes: Background hum, bells mark time, no investigation\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nRefugee testimony recorded: Fire purges (Halrick), mercy collapses (Mourne), ideological coordination confirmed\n\nTiffani’s tracking map revealed: Jhace now aware of pattern, partnership transparency increased\n\nBram recruited for sabotage recognition training: Crews taught to identify patterned stress-risers\n\nStrategic shift: From reactive repairs to proactive resistance—distributed networks, technique dissemination planned\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nSocial: Refugee burden—Dregs must absorb twelve additional families, straining resources\n\nEmotional: Tiffani confesses months-long secrecy, risks trust fracture (mitigated by Jhace’s acceptance)\n\nStrategic: Pattern recognition creates moral obligation—Jhace and Tiffani now responsible for countering ideological spread, not just local repairs\n\nTime: Preparation requires training hours, reduces immediate repair capacity\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nDawn: Refugees arrive, Tiffani investigates\n\nMorning: Tiffani interviews Halrick and Mourne families, updates map\n\nNight: Map revealed to Jhace, partnership transparency conversation\n\nSeventh-eighth bell: Jhace and Tiffani discuss strategic response\n\nNew Constraint (Persists 2–3 Chapters): Ideological threat confirmed—Jhace and Tiffani now face not just civic enforcement, but coordinated doctrinal movements spreading across cities (Mobel’s fire purges, Eries’s mercy collapses, Corlexi’s perfect districts, Salee’s binding communes). This shifts their work from reactive repairs to proactive resistance—they must now teach sabotage recognition, build distributed networks, and prepare for ideological escalation beyond structural failures. In Chapter 13 (The Dome Holds), this constraint surfaces when Jhace attempts to convince other districts to adopt distributed techniques, facing skepticism and resistance. In Chapter 14 (The List Arrives), Sanitist persecution escalates specifically targeting trained workers, confirming the ideology vs. craft conflict. Additionally, Tiffani’s revelation creates transparency obligation—Jhace now expects full honesty, so future secrets (if any) risk complete partnership collapse.\n\nRomance Micro-Act (Cluster 5, Act 1 of 2): Tiffani chooses to reveal tracking map (vulnerability through transparency). Jhace chooses to accept without blame (trust through forgiveness). Both choose continued partnership—hands intertwined, heads together, commitment reaffirmed despite escalating threat.\n\nCHAPTER 13 — The Dome Holds\n\nJhace POV\n\nThe dome is finished at dawn.\n\nJhace stands in the market square, neck craned, watching morning light catch the stress-traces—faint now, redistributed, stable. Forty-three ribs. Four weeks of work. Three sabotage attempts. Seventeen workers trained. Two arrested. One deported.\n\nBut the dome holds.\n\nMrs. Kettner is already setting up her stall—bread loaves arranged in neat rows, steam rising in the cold air. Kylar hauls crates. Chloen weaves between buyers, red scarf bright against cobblestones. The market opens like nothing happened. Like the glass above them wasn’t almost shattered three times.\n\nThe Dregs survive. Because they always do.\n\nJhace flexes his left hand—the functional one. His right is wrapped in bandages so thick he can barely bend the fingers. Two are completely numb. The healer warned him: One more overextension and you’ll lose the hand entirely.\n\nHe’s been working with his left for three days now. It’s slower. Shakier. But it works.\n\nTiffani is gone. Deported six days ago. No word since.\n\nJhace doesn’t know if she’s coming back. Doesn’t know if she can come back—Outer Crown residents need civic permits to enter Underface districts, and hers expired weeks before the raid. The Sanitists made sure everyone knew that.\n\nHe misses her. The way she moved through repairs—precise, methodical, no wasted motion. The way her rib scar pulled tight when she overextended, the way she pressed her palm against it and kept working anyway. The way she threaded her fingers through his in the dark, refusing to let him carry the weight alone.\n\nHe hasn’t slept properly since she left.\n\n“You finished it.”\n\nJhace turns. Bram stands three meters away, carrying his tactile board. The older rigger looks exhausted—gray hair, scarred hands, eyes that’ve seen too many collapses.\n\n“We finished it,” Jhace corrects.\n\n“She did most of the work.” Bram gestures at the dome. “The advanced stuff. Stasis locks. Redistribution matrices. That’s Outer Crown mastery.”\n\n“She taught me.”\n\n“Some of it.” Bram steps closer. “But you’re not her yet. And you won’t be if you keep shredding your hands trying to hold everything alone.”\n\nJhace doesn’t respond. He knows Bram’s right. Knows he’s been overcompensating since Tiffani left—taking on repairs she would’ve handled, teaching techniques he barely understands, pushing past safe limits because stopping means admitting they lost.\n\n“New posting arrived this morning,” Bram says quietly, pulling a folded paper from his belt. “Brighlee asked me to deliver it. She didn’t want to be seen.”\n\nJhace takes the paper, unfolding it. Tactile glyphs carved into heavy stock—bell mark, quill square, ring glyph, stamp bite.\n\nAnd names.\n\nTwelve names marked for civic review. Jhace’s is at the top. Bram’s is crossed out—not removed, just… marked. Like someone’s keeping track.\n\n“What does the cross-out mean?” Jhace asks.\n\n“Deferred review. They’re not arresting me yet. But they’re watching.” Bram’s voice flattens. “Kylar’s name is there. Selenthe’s. Three apprentices. Anyone who worked on the dome.”\n\nJhace stares at the list. Twelve workers. Some skilled. Some barely trained. All marked because they refused to let the market collapse.\n\nGood intentions aren’t an engineering property.\n\nHe learned that five years ago. Learns it again every time he bleeds into structures that should’ve been abandoned.\n\n“What do we do?” Jhace asks.\n\n“Strategic retreat.” Bram meets his gaze. “We stop the public repairs. Move operations underground. Continue the work quietly, without drawing Sanitist attention.”\n\n“That’s hiding.”\n\n“That’s surviving.” Bram’s tone sharpens. “You can’t hold the Dregs if you’re detained. And you can’t teach others if you’re dead.”\n\nJhace looks at the dome—stable, held, two hundred people trading beneath glass that almost shattered. He thinks about Tiffani’s words: If you try to save everything, you’ll save nothing. Including yourself.\n\nHe hated that logic. Still does.\n\nBut she was right.\n\n“Fine,” Jhace says quietly. “We go underground. But we don’t stop.”\n\n“I wouldn’t expect anything else.” Bram almost smiles. “There’s one more thing.”\n\n“What?”\n\n“Brighlee said a woman arrived at the northern gate this morning. Outer Crown accent. Carrying craft tools. Asked for you by name.”\n\nJhace’s pulse quickens. “Tiffani?”\n\n“Brighlee didn’t say. But she left coordinates for a meeting point. Western alcoves. Dusk.”\n\nJhace takes the coordinates, committing them to memory. “Thank you.”\n\nBram nods and walks away, disappearing into the market crowd.\n\nJhace stands in the square, staring at the dome. It holds. For now.\n\nThat has to be enough.\n\n\n\nDusk comes slowly. Jhace spends the day coordinating underground repairs—showing Kylar how to redistribute stress through dormant channels, teaching two apprentices basic velding, checking Mrs. Kettner’s stairs for the third time this week.\n\nThe work continues. Quietly. Invisibly.\n\nWhen the bells ring sixth hour, Jhace walks to the western alcoves. The space is narrow—stone walls, rusted pipes, shadows deep enough to hide. He waits, breath misting in the cold.\n\nFootsteps. Light. Familiar.\n\nTiffani emerges from shadow, carrying her satchel and stylus case. She looks exhausted—dark circles under her eyes, rib scar visible through torn shirt, hands wrapped in fresh bandages.\n\nBut she’s here.\n\n“You came back,” Jhace says.\n\n“I said I would.” Tiffani steps closer. “Though getting past the northern gate required… creative permit negotiation.”\n\n“Bribery?”\n\n“Strategic resource allocation.” She almost smiles. “How’s the dome?”\n\n“Stable. Finished three days ago.” Jhace flexes his left hand. “We lost two workers to arrests. Three more are under review.”\n\n“The Sanitists are escalating.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\nTiffani sets down her satchel, pulling out a folded map. “I need to show you something.”\n\nShe spreads the map on cobblestones. Four cities marked—Halrick, Mourne, Vieln, Odern. Burn sites. Collapse zones. Perfect districts. Healing communes. Refugee accounts correlated with locations.\n\n“While I was in Strathmoor, I confirmed the pattern,” Tiffani says. “Fire purges in Halrick. Mercy collapses in Mourne. Workers fainting in Vieln. Patients binding in Odern. It’s not random. Someone’s spreading doctrines across cities—ideologies that feel like craft but operate differently.”\n\nJhace stares at the map. “How many cities?”\n\n“Four confirmed. Probably more.” Tiffani traces the burn sites. “And it’s accelerating. Halrick burned six districts in three weeks. Mourne collapsed twelve blocks. Vieln’s perfect geometry has spread to adjacent regions.”\n\n“What do we do?”\n\n“We prepare.” Tiffani folds the map. “Teach workers to recognize sabotage patterns. Build distributed networks so if one district falls, others hold. Document techniques so knowledge survives even if we don’t.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a moment. Then he asks, “Do you think we can stop it?”\n\n“No.” Tiffani meets his gaze. “But we can slow it. And we can make sure someone else picks up the work when we’re gone.”\n\nJhace reaches out, threading his left hand through hers. “I’m glad you’re back.”\n\n“Me too.” Tiffani squeezes his hand gently. “How’s your right hand?”\n\n“Not good. Healer says one more overextension and I lose function permanently.”\n\n“Then you don’t overextend.” Her voice is firm. “We distribute the load. You teach, I execute. Together.”\n\n“Like always.”\n\n“Like always.”\n\nThey stand in silence, hands intertwined, listening to the heat pipes hum beneath cobblestones. The bells ring seventh hour. The market closes. The Dregs settle into evening routines.\n\n“There’s something else,” Tiffani says quietly.\n\n“What?”\n\n“The refugee accounts. Fire purges, mercy collapses, perfect districts, binding communes.” She hesitates. “The people spreading these doctrines—Mobel, Eries, Corlexi, Salee—they’re not just skilled practitioners. They’re something more. Their craft doesn’t cost them the way it costs us. And their followers speak about them like… prophets.”\n\nJhace frowns. “What are you saying?”\n\n“I’m saying this isn’t just civic enforcement or ideological movement. It’s…” She trails off, searching for words. “I don’t know what it is yet. But it’s bigger than we thought.”\n\nJhace processes that. He thinks about the dome sabotage—patterned stress-risers carved with precision that suggested advanced craft training. Thinks about the Sanitist raids—coordinated, escalating, justified through doctrine that sounds religious.\n\n“What do you do when the city doesn’t just break,” Tiffani says quietly. “But chooses to?”\n\nJhace doesn’t have an answer. He just holds her hand tighter.\n\nEventually, they leave the alcove, walking back toward the storage room they’ve been using as a base. The Dregs are quiet—workers resting, children sleeping, heat pipes glowing faint beneath streets.\n\nThe world is broken. But for now, it holds.\n\n\n\nThat night, they lie in the storage room, side by side, hands intertwined in the dark.\n\n“I missed you,” Jhace says quietly.\n\n“I missed you too.” Tiffani shifts closer, resting her head against his shoulder. “Six days felt like months.”\n\n“What did you do in Strathmoor?”\n\n“Confirmed patterns. Gathered intelligence. Negotiated permits.” She exhales slowly. “And tried not to think about you overextending without me here to stop you.”\n\nJhace almost smiles. “I didn’t overextend.”\n\n“Liar.”\n\n“Okay. Once. Maybe twice.”\n\nTiffani reaches over, touching his bandaged right hand gently. “This has to stop.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“Do you?” Her voice softens. “Because you keep saying that, and then you keep bleeding.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a moment. Then he says, “I don’t know how to stop. The work doesn’t stop. The collapses don’t stop. If I quit, people die.”\n\n“If you kill yourself, people die anyway.” Tiffani threads her fingers through his left hand. “You can’t hold the city alone. You have to let others carry weight.”\n\n“I’m trying.”\n\n“I know.” She squeezes his hand. “And you’re doing better. Kylar’s leading crews now. Selenthe’s teaching apprentices. Bram’s coordinating underground repairs. You built that. That’s the work.”\n\nJhace closes his eyes, feeling the tremor in his left hand—chronic now, probably permanent. “The dome’s finished.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“But the list arrived. Sanitists are marking us for review.”\n\n“I know. Brighlee told me.” Tiffani’s voice hardens slightly. “Which is why we go underground. Strategic retreat. We survive long enough to teach the next generation.”\n\n“And then?”\n\n“Then they hold the line. And we rest.”\n\nJhace turns his head, looking at her in the darkness. “Do you really think we’ll survive this?”\n\n“I think we’ll hold as long as we can.” Tiffani leans closer, pressing her forehead against his. “And that’s enough.”\n\nThey lie in silence, breathing the same air, hands intertwined. Outside, the heat pipes hum. The bells ring eighth hour. The Dregs sleep—or try to.\n\nThe world continues. Broken, but functional.\n\nJhace closes his eyes, feeling Tiffani’s warmth against him, and thinks: We finished the dome. We taught the workers. We held the line.\n\nFor now, that’s enough.\n\nTomorrow, they’ll hold again.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 13\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nDome repair completed: Forty-three ribs stabilized, market reopens, structure holds (major A-plot success)\n\nTiffani returns: Deported six days ago, returns via “creative permit negotiation,” reunites with Jhace\n\nNew civic list: Twelve names marked for review—Jhace (top), Bram (cross-out/deferred), Kylar, Selenthe, apprentices (collective targeted)\n\nTracking map expanded: Tiffani confirms four-city pattern in Strathmoor—burn sites, collapse zones, perfect districts, healing communes\n\nHeat pipes: Background hum, bells mark time, no investigation\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nMarket dome: Repair completed, stress-traces stabilized, structure fully functional (first major success)\n\nCivic review list: Twelve workers marked, Bram’s name crossed out (deferred arrest), Sanitist surveillance escalated\n\nTiffani’s intelligence: Four-city pattern confirmed—Mobel (Halrick), Eries (Mourne), Corlexi (Vieln), Salee (Odern) identified as ideological leaders\n\nStrategic retreat decision: Underground repairs adopted, public work suspended (tactical shift)\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nBody: Jhace—right hand deteriorating (two fingers numb, healer warning reinforced), left hand tremor worsening\n\nBody: Tiffani—rib scar visible through torn shirt, exhaustion from six-day absence and intelligence gathering\n\nSocial: Two workers arrested during Tiffani’s absence, three under review (collective attrition continues)\n\nSocial: Strategic retreat reduces visible Dregs resistance, creates perception of defeat (morale cost)\n\nTime: Six-day separation delays coordination, reduces repair efficiency\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nDawn: Dome completion confirmed, market reopens\n\nMidday: Bram delivers civic review list, coordinates Tiffani’s return\n\nSixth bell: Jhace coordinates underground repairs\n\nDusk: Jhace meets Tiffani at western alcoves, map revealed\n\nSeventh-eighth bell: Return to storage room, partnership reaffirmed\n\nNew Constraint (Persists 2–3 Chapters): Strategic retreat adopted—Jhace and Tiffani shift from public repairs to underground operations, reducing visibility but also reducing community engagement. This creates distributed leadership pressure—Kylar, Bram, and Selenthe must now lead independently without constant oversight, increasing risk of mistakes and miscommunication. In Chapter 14 (The List Arrives), this constraint surfaces when a crew makes a critical error without Jhace present, causing injury. In Chapter 15 (The Line That Holds), the constraint forces Jhace to accept he cannot supervise every repair, requiring trust in undertrained workers. Additionally, Tiffani’s intelligence revelation creates ideological awareness—Jhace now knows the conflict isn’t just civic enforcement but doctrinal spread. This shifts their work from reactive repairs to proactive resistance preparation, adding strategic planning burden to already-exhausted practitioners.\n\nRomance Micro-Act (Cluster 5, Act 2 of 2): Jhace chooses to wait for Tiffani despite uncertainty (trust through patience). Tiffani chooses to return despite deportation risk (commitment despite danger). Both choose physical reunion—hands intertwined, forehead to forehead, partnership reaffirmed through presence after separation.\n\nCHAPTER 14 — The List Arrives\n\nTiffani POV\n\nThe injury happens at second bell.\n\nTiffani is coordinating three crews—Bram’s team on the northern scaffold, Kylar supervising apprentices at a tenement stairwell, Selenthe teaching two new workers basic velding in the western alcoves. She’s moving between sites, checking progress, correcting mistakes before they become collapses.\n\nUnderground work. Strategic retreat. No public repairs that draw Sanitist attention.\n\nIt’s working. Barely.\n\nShe hears the scream from two blocks away—high-pitched, cut short. She runs.\n\nBy the time she reaches the tenement stairwell, Kylar is kneeling beside an apprentice—young man named Corvin, maybe nineteen, blood soaking through his shirt. A support beam has collapsed, pinning his leg.\n\n“What happened?” Tiffani drops beside them, assessing the injury. Broken femur. Maybe worse.\n\n“He tried to hold the beam alone,” Kylar says, voice tight. “Didn’t call for backup. Forced Wholeness into a vein he couldn’t read properly. It detonated.”\n\nTiffani stares at Corvin. He’s shaking, pale, breath shallow from shock. His hands are bleeding—fresh tendon splits from overextension.\n\n“Where’s Jhace?” Corvin asks, voice barely audible.\n\n“At the market dome,” Tiffani says. “Teaching stasis lock techniques to Bram’s crew.”\n\n“I thought—” Corvin coughs, blood flecking his lips. “I thought I could do it. Like he does.”\n\nTiffani’s stomach twists. This is what happens when you teach desperate people advanced techniques without proper supervision. They watch Jhace bleed himself empty holding scaffolds, and they think that’s normal. That’s what craft is supposed to cost.\n\n“Don’t move,” Tiffani says. She presses her hands against his leg, finding the vein. The bone is shattered—stress channels rupturing faster than she can stabilize them.\n\nShe needs Jhace. His Wholeness. Her Form. Together, they could hold this long enough for a healer to arrive.\n\nBut Jhace isn’t here.\n\nTiffani makes a choice.\n\nShe threads Form into the fractured bone, locking it in geometric suspension. Not healing—just freezing it mid-break, buying time. The cost hits immediately—her ribs scream, old scar pulling tight, new vein burns branching outward.\n\nShe doesn’t stop.\n\n“Kylar,” she says through gritted teeth. “Get a healer. Now.”\n\nKylar runs.\n\nTiffani holds the stasis lock, breath shallow, sweat sheeting down her face despite the cold. Corvin stares at her, eyes wide.\n\n“I’m sorry,” he whispers. “I thought—”\n\n“Don’t.” Tiffani’s voice is flat. “You thought you could work like Jhace. You can’t. He’s been doing this for years, and it’s killing him. You tried it once and shattered your leg.”\n\nCorvin flinches.\n\n“That’s not a criticism,” Tiffani says, softer. “It’s a fact. You’re not ready for solo work yet. And that’s not failure. That’s just where you are.”\n\nThe healer arrives—older woman, scarred hands, tactile board tucked under her arm. She assesses Corvin quickly, then looks at Tiffani.\n\n“How long can you hold the lock?” she asks.\n\n“Five minutes. Maybe less.”\n\n“I need ten.”\n\nTiffani exhales slowly. “Then work fast.”\n\nThe healer kneels, pulling bone splints and bandages from her satchel. She works with sharp efficiency—stabilizing the femur, wrapping the leg, injecting something that makes Corvin’s eyes glaze over.\n\nTiffani holds the stasis lock, counting seconds. Her ribs are on fire. The vein burns are spreading—she can feel them branching beneath her shirt, hot and immediate.\n\nAt eight minutes, the healer finishes. “Release.”\n\nTiffani pulls her hands back, gasping. The lock collapses, but the bone is stabilized enough. Corvin won’t die. He won’t walk properly for months, but he’ll survive.\n\nThe healer stands, dusting off her knees. “He’ll need rest. Weeks. Maybe longer.”\n\n“Understood.” Tiffani pushes herself upright, legs shaking.\n\nThe healer glances at Tiffani’s shirt—blood soaking through where the vein burns have split skin. “You should let me treat that.”\n\n“Later.”\n\n“Now.”\n\nTiffani doesn’t argue. She sits against the wall while the healer unwraps her shirt, exposing the rib scar and the fresh burns branching from it. The healer’s expression doesn’t change, but Tiffani sees the calculation in her eyes.\n\n“You’re using stasis lock too often,” the healer says quietly.\n\n“I know.”\n\n“It’s killing you. Slowly.”\n\n“I know.”\n\nThe healer wraps the burns with clean cloth, tying it tight. “Stop using it.”\n\n“Can’t.”\n\n“Why not?”\n\n“Because people keep breaking, and we’re the only ones who can hold them together.” Tiffani stands, adjusting her shirt. “How long until Corvin recovers?”\n\n“Six weeks minimum. Longer if he overextends again.”\n\n“He won’t.” Tiffani meets the healer’s gaze. “I’ll make sure of it.”\n\nThe healer nods and leaves, taking Corvin on a makeshift stretcher.\n\nTiffani stands in the empty stairwell, staring at the collapsed beam. This is what happens when you distribute the load without proper training. When you teach people to hold the line before they’re ready.\n\nShe thinks about Dren, overextending at the northern tenement. Thinks about Corvin, trying to work like Jhace. Thinks about the twelve names on the civic review list—all of them trained in techniques they barely understand, all of them risking permanent injury because that’s what survival requires.\n\nThe math doesn’t balance. It never did.\n\n\n\nThat night, Tiffani finds Jhace at the market dome. He’s teaching Bram how to perform basic stasis locks—not the advanced matrix Tiffani uses, just simple geometric freezes that buy time during repairs.\n\nShe waits until the lesson ends, then approaches.\n\n“Corvin got injured,” she says without preamble.\n\nJhace’s expression shifts. “How bad?”\n\n“Broken femur. Overextended trying to hold a beam alone. Healer says six weeks recovery minimum.”\n\nJhace closes his eyes briefly. “Where was his supervisor?”\n\n“Kylar. But Kylar was two blocks away, coordinating another crew.” Tiffani steps closer. “This is what happens when we distribute the load, Jhace. We teach people techniques they’re not ready for, and they hurt themselves trying to work like we do.”\n\n“Then what do you suggest?” His voice is tight, controlled. “We can’t hold the Dregs alone. You said that yourself.”\n\n“I know.” Tiffani exhales slowly. “But we can’t keep teaching advanced techniques to apprentices who don’t understand the cost. Dren shredded his hands. Corvin shattered his leg. How many more before we admit this isn’t sustainable?”\n\nJhace doesn’t respond immediately. He just stands there, flexing his left hand—the functional one. His right is wrapped in bandages so thick he can’t bend the fingers.\n\n“What do you want me to do?” he asks finally. “Stop teaching? Let the structures fall?”\n\n“No. Teach slower. Better. Focus on fundamentals—basic bracing, simple redistribution. Save the advanced work for people who are ready.”\n\n“That takes time we don’t have.”\n\n“I know.” Tiffani reaches out, touching his bandaged hand gently. “But if we keep pushing apprentices past their limits, we’ll lose them. Permanently. And then we’ll have no one.”\n\nJhace stares at her hand on his. Then he nods, once. “Fine. We adjust the training. Focus on basics. No stasis locks for apprentices.”\n\n“Agreed.”\n\nThey stand in silence, listening to the heat pipes hum beneath cobblestones. The market is empty—vendors gone, stalls folded, glass dome glowing faint violet in the darkness.\n\n“There’s something else,” Tiffani says quietly.\n\n“What?”\n\nShe pulls a folded paper from her belt. “New list. Brighlee delivered it this afternoon.”\n\nJhace takes the paper, unfolding it. Tactile glyphs carved deep—bell mark, quill square, ring glyph, stamp bite.\n\nTwenty names.\n\nJhace’s is at the top. Tiffani’s beside it. Bram’s name is crossed out again—deferred review, but marked. Kylar. Selenthe. Eight apprentices. Three vendors who donated supplies. Mrs. Kettner.\n\n“Mrs. Kettner?” Jhace’s voice is hollow. “She’s seventy years old. She bakes bread.”\n\n“She housed refugees,” Tiffani says. “The families from Halrick and Mourne. That’s aiding illegal residents. The Sanitists marked her.”\n\nJhace stares at the list, hands trembling. “They’re targeting everyone. Not just workers. Everyone who helped.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“How long until they escalate?”\n\n“Days. Maybe hours.” Tiffani takes the list back, folding it carefully. “Brighlee said there’s a second document coming. Full eviction orders. Not quotas. Not review. Just clearance.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a long moment. Then he asks, “What do we do?”\n\n“We prepare. Document everything. Teach workers to recognize Sanitist tactics. Build escape routes. Distribute supplies so no one depends on a single source.” Tiffani meets his gaze. “And we accept that we might not win this.”\n\n“I don’t accept that.”\n\n“Jhace—”\n\n“No.” His voice hardens. “I’ve spent five years holding this district together. I’m not letting the Sanitists burn it because we ran out of time.”\n\nTiffani wants to argue. Wants to shake him until he understands that refusal to quit isn’t the same as capacity to win. But she’s too exhausted, and she knows he won’t listen.\n\n“Then we hold as long as we can,” she says finally. “Together.”\n\nJhace reaches out, threading his left hand through hers. “Always.”\n\n\n\nThey walk back to the storage room in silence. The Dregs are quiet—workers resting, children sleeping, heat pipes glowing faint beneath streets. The world continues, broken but functional.\n\nWhen they reach the storage room, Tiffani collapses on the bedroll, too exhausted to undress. Jhace sits beside her, staring at his bandaged right hand.\n\n“I can’t feel three fingers now,” he says quietly.\n\nTiffani turns her head, looking at him. “The healer warned you.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“You need to stop using that hand.”\n\n“I know.”\n\n“But you won’t.”\n\nJhace is quiet for a moment. Then he says, “I can’t. Not while people are still breaking.”\n\nTiffani reaches out, touching his face gently. “You’re breaking too.”\n\n“I’ll heal.”\n\n“No. You won’t.” Her voice softens. “That’s the cost. Permanent damage. And you keep paying it.”\n\nJhace leans into her touch, closing his eyes. “I don’t know how to stop.”\n\n“I know.” Tiffani shifts closer, resting her forehead against his. “But I’m here. And I’ll carry what you can’t.”\n\nThey lie down together, hands intertwined in the dark. Tiffani feels the tremor in his left hand—chronic now, probably permanent. She feels her own ribs ache, vein burns spreading beneath bandages.\n\nThey’re both breaking. Slowly. Inevitably.\n\nBut for now, they hold.\n\nOutside, the bells ring eighth hour. The heat pipes hum. The Dregs sleep—or try to.\n\nAnd Tiffani thinks: We taught them to hold the line. Now we have to teach them when to let go.\n\nTomorrow, she’ll burn the new list. Tomorrow, she’ll adjust the training protocols. Tomorrow, she’ll prepare for the evictions that are coming.\n\nBut tonight, she just holds Jhace’s hand and listens to him breathe.\n\nThe world is broken. But they’re still here.\n\nThat has to be enough.\n\n\n\nCONTINUITY LEDGER — CHAPTER 14\n\nPosted Clauses Used:\n\nCorvin injured: Apprentice overextends, breaks femur attempting solo beam repair (distributed leadership failure)\n\nTiffani uses stasis lock: Holds Corvin’s shattered bone for eight minutes, vein burns spread and bleed\n\nNew civic list: Twenty names marked—Jhace, Tiffani, Bram (crossed out), Kylar, Selenthe, eight apprentices, three vendors, Mrs. Kettner (elderly tenant)\n\nTraining adjustment proposed: Focus on fundamentals, no stasis locks for apprentices (strategic shift)\n\nHeat pipes: Background hum, bells mark time, no investigation\n\nArtifacts Created/Altered:\n\nCorvin removed from active repairs: Broken femur, six-week recovery minimum (crew attrition continues)\n\nNew civic list (twenty names): Escalation from twelve to twenty—targets expanded to include vendors, refugees, elderly\n\nTraining protocol adjustment: Jhace and Tiffani agree to slow down, teach basics only, save advanced work for ready practitioners\n\nTiffani’s vein burns: Fresh marks bleeding through shirt, healer warns against continued stasis lock use\n\nCosts (Body/Social/Coin):\n\nBody: Corvin—broken femur, tendon splits, removed from work (weeks/months recovery)\n\nBody: Tiffani—vein burns spread and bleed, healer explicitly warns stasis lock is killing her\n\nBody: Jhace—three fingers numb (right hand), function declining toward total paralysis\n\nSocial: Mrs. Kettner marked for civic review—elderly tenant targeted for housing refugees (collective persecution escalates)\n\nSocial: Twenty workers/vendors marked—attrition pressure compounds, fear spreads\n\nTime/Bell Markers:\n\nSecond bell: Corvin injured at tenement stairwell\n\nMidday: Healer treats Corvin, Tiffani’s burns wrapped\n\nEvening: Jhace teaching Bram at market dome\n\nNight: Tiffani delivers new list, training adjustment discussion\n\nEighth bell: Return to storage room, exhaustion\n\nNew Constraint (Persists Through Chapter 15 & Epilogue): Training protocol shift—Jhace and Tiffani abandon accelerated advanced technique training, focusing only on fundamentals (basic bracing, simple redistribution). This creates capacity reduction—fewer workers can perform delicate repairs, concentrating advanced work back onto Jhace and Tiffani despite their declining physical capacity. In Chapter 15 (The Line That Holds), this constraint forces Jhace to attempt a critical repair alone when no trained worker is available, nearly causing his death. Additionally, twenty-name civic list creates persecution escalation—Sanitists now target not just workers but vendors, refugees, elderly supporters. This confirms ideological conflict (doctrine vs. craft) and sets up N2’s broader persecution\nThe file is too long and its contents have been truncated.",

Key lines preserved; connective phrasing tightened for clarity.\n\n---\n\n### 211 — User\n“Ok now for **novella one** give me **everything needed to drop in** to create a **custom GPT** — include **voice, tone**, everything.”\n\n### 212 — Assistant\nDelivered **Custom GPT: Novella-1 Handoff Pack**:\n- **Voice/Tone:** grounded, procedural intimacy; zero purple prose; consent shown, not sermonized; **no foreshadowing**; anti-thesis lines (no “paper moves bodies” slogans).\n- **World Rails:** forge/glass lexicon; **no harmonics**; permitted sensory: metal, heat, mist, matte, ridges; time by **bells**; public law via ridged postings.\n- **POV:** strict dual (Jhace/Tiffani), deep focus, no authorial wink.\n- **Magic Ladder:** set → trace → veil → countermark → pressure math → field audit; costs tracked (tremor, thirst, ear-ring, breath/sleep debt).\n- **Don’ts:** no ledger text inside prose; no god/mask truths; no Auditor/Counterspark hints; no musical metaphors for magic.\n- **Targets:** 15 chapters, **900–1500 words** each; each ends on a **proof-of-change** image.\n\n### 213 — User\n“**Yeah do that.**”\n\n### 214 — Assistant\nConfirmed and shipped the **N1 Handoff Pack** bundle (voice + rails + allowed lexicon + chapter targets + example closing images).\n\n---\n\n### 215 — User\n“This was the **first response**”  \nPastes a long **Chapter 1 draft** (title banner “Lines That Hold — Te’Oga — Novella 1”; scene: **‘Wet Trace at Blackgate’** with Jhace, Tiffani, Brighlee, lane, droplet icon, wedges, veil, posting, costs).\n\n### 216 — Assistant\nHigh-level read:\n- Excellent **procedural choreography** and tactile law.\n- Over target length; some **meta thesis** lines creep in; ledger language bleeding into prose.\n\n### 217 — User\n**Critique (your words\n"
